name: Science Literacy
description: Understand how the world works. Physics, chemistry, biology, and the scientific method.

nodes.name: The Scientific Method
nodes.description: Hypothesis, experiment, conclusion. How we know what we know.
nodes.whyItMatters: The scientific method is humanity's best tool for finding truth — and it works just as well for testing complex strategies as it does for curing diseases. Every time you form a hypothesis ('this intervention improves outcomes'), run an experiment (test on 100 observations), and analyze the results, you're using the scientific method. Without it, you're just guessing. With it, you replace bias with evidence. It protects you from misinformation, pseudoscience, and your own cognitive biases that cost real money.
practiceProblems: Design an experiment to test if plants grow better with music
Identify independent/dependent variables in: 'Does caffeine improve test scores?'
What's wrong with: 'I saw it on TikTok so it must be true'?
Explain the difference between correlation and causation using a real-world example

resources.title: Crash Course: Scientific Method
resources.whyHelpful: Concise 10-minute overview of the scientific method. Perfect quick refresher before diving into experimental design and data analysis.

resources.title: Scientific Method (Khan Academy)
resources.whyHelpful: Complete Khan Academy unit with interactive exercises and quizzes. Best for hands-on practice with experimental design concepts.

resources.title: Skepticism and Critical Thinking (TED-Ed)
resources.whyHelpful: Collection of TED-Ed talks on critical thinking. Useful for understanding why the scientific method matters in everyday life and media consumption.

resources.title: Crash Course: Statistics (Scientific Thinking)
resources.whyHelpful: Explains how data analysis and statistics are used in real scientific research — connects math skills to science literacy.

resources.title: Understanding Science (UC Berkeley)
resources.whyHelpful: Interactive website that explains how science really works — peer review, bias, reproducibility. Essential for understanding the scientific process beyond the textbook version.

workedExamples.problem: You read a headline: 'Study finds coffee causes cancer in rats.' Using the scientific method, what questions should you ask before believing this?
workedExamples.solution: 1. What was the sample size? 2. Was there a control group? 3. How much coffee did the rats consume (dose)? 4. Has the study been peer reviewed? 5. Can the results be replicated? 6. Do rat studies apply to humans? Without answers, the headline is meaningless.
workedExamples.explanation: The scientific method demands skepticism. A single study, especially in animals, rarely proves anything in humans. Sample size, controls, peer review, and replication are what separate real science from clickbait.

workedExamples.problem: A company claims their supplement "boosts memory by 300%." Design a controlled experiment to test this claim.
workedExamples.solution: 1. Randomly assign participants to treatment (supplement) and control (placebo) groups. 2. Use double-blind design. 3. Measure memory with standardized tests before and after. 4. Control for age, education, sleep, and other variables. 5. Use statistical analysis to determine if the difference is significant. 6. Have other labs replicate the study.
workedExamples.explanation: A proper experiment needs randomization, a control group, double-blinding, and statistical significance. Without these, the claim is just marketing.

workedExamples.problem: A study claims that people who eat chocolate daily live longer. Headline: 'Chocolate Extends Lifespan!' The study surveyed 500 people who self-reported eating chocolate. Critique this study and design a better one.
workedExamples.solution: Flaws: (1) Self-reported data is unreliable. (2) Correlation ≠ causation — chocolate eaters might also exercise more or be wealthier. (3) No control group. (4) No randomization. (5) Survey bias — people who believe chocolate is healthy might overreport eating it. Better design: Randomly assign 1000 people to daily chocolate or placebo group for 10 years, track mortality rates with medical records, control for diet/wealth/exercise confounders.
workedExamples.explanation: Observational studies can find correlations but can't prove causation. The gold standard for causal claims is a randomized controlled trial (RCT). When reading health headlines, always ask: was this an RCT or an observational study? Confounding variables often explain the apparent effect.

commonMistakes: Confusing correlation with causation — just because two things happen together doesn't mean one caused the other. Always look for confounding variables.
Assuming a single study proves a claim — science requires replication. One study, especially a small one, is just a data point, not a conclusion.
Thinking a hypothesis and a theory are the same — a hypothesis is an untested prediction; a theory is a well-tested framework supported by mountains of evidence.

quiz.question: In a controlled experiment testing a new drug, what is the purpose of the control group?
options: To receive a placebo to compare against the treatment group
To serve as the group that designs the experiment
quiz.explanation: The control group receives a placebo (or no treatment) to provide a baseline for comparison. Without a control group, you can't know if changes in the treatment group are caused by the drug or by other factors (placebo effect, natural recovery).

quiz.question: A study finds that ice cream sales and shark attacks both increase in summer. What's the most likely relationship?
options: Shark attacks cause people to buy ice cream
A confounding variable (hot weather) causes both
There is no relationship, it's a coincidence
quiz.explanation: This is a classic correlation ≠ causation example. Hot weather is a confounding variable that causes both more ice cream sales AND more people swimming in the ocean (leading to more shark attacks). The headline 'Ice cream causes shark attacks' would be dangerously misleading.

quiz.question: Why is peer review essential to the scientific method?
options: It's a formality required for publication
It catches errors, bias, and methodological flaws before research enters public knowledge
Peer review guarantees a study is correct
quiz.explanation: Peer review is a quality control mechanism where independent experts evaluate a study's methodology, data, and conclusions. It catches flaws the original researchers missed. However, peer review doesn't guarantee correctness — replication is the real gold standard.

quiz.question: What is the key difference between a hypothesis and a scientific theory?
options: A hypothesis is an untested guess while a theory is supported by extensive evidence
A theory is less reliable than a hypothesis
quiz.explanation: A hypothesis is a testable prediction that has not yet been extensively tested. A scientific theory (like evolution or relativity) has been confirmed through repeated testing and evidence. Theories don't 'graduate' into facts — they are frameworks that explain facts.

nodes.name: Waves & Light
nodes.description: Sound waves, electromagnetic spectrum, and the nature of light.
keyConcepts: Wave properties: amplitude, wavelength, frequency, period
nodes.whyItMatters: Waves carry information across the universe. Radio, WiFi, visible light, X-rays — they're all electromagnetic waves. Understanding waves is understanding communication.
practiceProblems: Frequency of wave with wavelength 2m traveling at 340 m/s
Why is the sky blue? (Rayleigh scattering)
Calculate frequency of red light (λ ≈ 700nm)
Explain Doppler effect with an ambulance siren

resources.title: Khan Academy: Waves
resources.whyHelpful: Khan Academy complete waves unit with exercises on wave properties, frequency, wavelength, and applications in sound and light.

resources.title: Light: Particle or Wave?
resources.whyHelpful: Wikipedia overview of light dual nature covering the wave-particle debate. Best for deep reference.

resources.title: Electromagnetic Spectrum
resources.whyHelpful: Comprehensive EM spectrum breakdown from radio to gamma rays. Essential for understanding WiFi, medicine, and astronomy applications.

resources.title: PhET Sims: Wave on a String
resources.whyHelpful: Interactive wave simulation — adjust frequency, wavelength, and amplitude to see wave motion visually. Great for understanding v = fλ.

resources.title: 3Blue1Brown: Wave Visualization
resources.whyHelpful: Stunning visual explanations of wave behavior — interference, diffraction, and Fourier decomposition. Makes abstract wave concepts intuitive.

workedExamples.problem: A sound wave travels at 340 m/s and has a wavelength of 2 m. What is its frequency? Then calculate the frequency of red light with wavelength 700 nm traveling at 3 × 10⁸ m/s.
workedExamples.solution: v = fλ → f = v/λ. For sound: f = 340/2 = 170 Hz. For light: f = (3 × 10⁸) / (700 × 10⁻⁹) ≈ 4.29 × 10¹⁴ Hz.
workedExamples.explanation: The wave equation v = fλ applies to all waves. Sound waves are mechanical (need a medium), light is electromagnetic (can travel through vacuum). Higher frequency means higher energy for electromagnetic waves.

workedExamples.problem: Light travels from air (n = 1.00) into water (n = 1.33) at an angle of 30° from the normal. Find the angle of refraction using Snell's law. What happens to the speed and wavelength of light in water?
workedExamples.solution: Snell's law: n₁ sin(θ₁) = n₂ sin(θ₂). 1.00 × sin(30°) = 1.33 × sin(θ₂). sin(θ₂) = 0.5/1.33 ≈ 0.376. θ₂ ≈ 22.1°. Speed in water: v = c/n = 3×10⁸/1.33 ≈ 2.26 × 10⁸ m/s. Wavelength decreases proportionally since frequency stays constant.
workedExamples.explanation: Light slows down in denser media, causing it to bend toward the normal. Frequency stays constant but wavelength shortens. This is why a straw looks bent in water — refraction changes the apparent position.

workedExamples.problem: An ambulance approaches you at 34 m/s emitting a 800 Hz siren. What frequency do you hear? Then: A wave on a string has wavelength 0.5 m and frequency 50 Hz. What is the wave speed? If the string is tightened and the wave speed doubles (frequency stays at 50 Hz), what is the new wavelength?
workedExamples.solution: Doppler effect: f' = f × v/(v-v_s) = 800 × 340/(340-34) = 800 × 1.111 ≈ 889 Hz. Wave speed: v = fλ = 50 × 0.5 = 25 m/s. After tightening: new wavelength = 50/50 = 1 m (doubles because speed doubles while f stays fixed).
workedExamples.explanation: The Doppler effect compresses waves when the source approaches, increasing the observed frequency. Wave speed on a string depends on tension — tighter strings transmit waves faster. Since frequency is determined by the source oscillator, wavelength must adjust when speed changes.

commonMistakes: Confusing wavelength and frequency — they are inversely related. Higher frequency means shorter wavelength.
Thinking all waves need a medium — sound does, but light (electromagnetic waves) travels through vacuum.
Forgetting unit conversions — nanometers to meters requires multiplying by 10⁻⁹. Always check your units.

quiz.question: Which type of wave can travel through a vacuum?
quiz.explanation: Light is an electromagnetic wave and does not require a medium. Sound, water, and seismic waves all need matter to propagate.

quiz.question: What happens to the wavelength of light as frequency increases?
quiz.explanation: From v = fλ, for a constant wave speed, wavelength and frequency are inversely proportional. Higher frequency means shorter wavelength.

quiz.question: Which part of the electromagnetic spectrum has the highest energy?
quiz.explanation: Higher frequency electromagnetic waves have higher energy. X-rays and gamma rays have the highest frequency and energy in the electromagnetic spectrum.

quiz.question: What is the Doppler effect?
options: Waves change speed when entering a new medium
Observed frequency changes when source and observer move relative to each other
Waves add together to make a louder sound
quiz.explanation: The Doppler effect is the change in frequency observed when the source of waves and the observer are moving relative to each other. This is why an ambulance siren sounds higher pitched as it approaches and lower as it moves away.

quiz.question: An ambulance with a siren emitting 800 Hz approaches you at 34 m/s. If the speed of sound is 340 m/s, what frequency do you hear? (Use f' = f × (v/(v-v_s)))
quiz.explanation: Using f' = f × (v/(v-v_s)) = 800 × (340/(340-34)) = 800 × (340/306) ≈ 800 × 1.111 ≈ 889 Hz. The frequency increases as the source approaches due to the Doppler effect.

nodes.name: Atoms & Elements
nodes.description: Atomic structure, periodic table, and chemical bonding basics.
nodes.whyItMatters: Everything is made of atoms. Chemistry explains why salt dissolves, why iron rusts, and how batteries work. It's the invisible world that shapes everything visible.
practiceProblems: How many protons, neutrons, electrons in Carbon-12?
What group do noble gases belong to?
Is NaCl ionic or covalent? Explain.
Draw electron configuration of Oxygen (atomic number 8)

resources.title: Khan Academy: Atoms
resources.whyHelpful: Khan Academy structured course on atomic structure, electron configurations, and the periodic table. Great for learning the fundamentals with practice problems.

resources.title: Periodic Table Videos
resources.whyHelpful: Video playlist where each element gets its own episode. Makes the periodic table memorable and fun.

resources.title: Atomic Structure (Chemistry LibreTexts)
resources.whyHelpful: Free online textbook covering atomic theory, quantum numbers, and electron configurations. More detailed than introductory courses.

resources.title: PhET Sims: Build an Atom
resources.whyHelpful: Drag protons, neutrons, and electrons to build atoms — see how atomic number, mass, and charge change as you add particles. Makes isotopes intuitive.

resources.title: WebElements: Periodic Table
resources.whyHelpful: Comprehensive interactive periodic table with properties, electron configurations, and isotope data for every element. Great quick reference tool.

workedExamples.problem: How many protons, neutrons, and electrons are in Carbon-14? What is its electron configuration? Is NaCl ionic or covalent?
workedExamples.solution: Carbon has atomic number 6, so 6 protons and 6 electrons. Carbon-14 has mass number 14, so neutrons = 14 - 6 = 8. Electron configuration: 2, 4. NaCl is ionic — sodium gives an electron to chlorine, forming Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions that attract each other.
workedExamples.explanation: Atomic number = protons. Mass number = protons + neutrons. Ionic bonds involve electron transfer between metals and nonmetals. Covalent bonds involve electron sharing, usually between nonmetals.

workedExamples.problem: Magnesium (atomic number 12) reacts with oxygen (atomic number 8) to form magnesium oxide. Write the balanced equation. What type of bond is formed? How many electrons does each atom gain or lose?
workedExamples.solution: Balanced: 2Mg + O₂ → 2MgO. Mg has 2 valence electrons and loses both to form Mg²⁺. O has 6 valence electrons and gains 2 to form O²⁻. This is an ionic bond. MgO is a white crystalline solid with a high melting point.
workedExamples.explanation: Metals lose electrons to form cations, nonmetals gain electrons to form anions. The transfer creates ions with full outer shells (stable octet). Ionic compounds have high melting points because the electrostatic forces between ions are very strong.

workedExamples.problem: A carbon atom (electronegativity 2.5) bonds with an oxygen atom (electronegativity 3.5). Is the C-O bond ionic, polar covalent, or nonpolar covalent? What about C-H (electronegativity 2.1)? Then: Why does CO2 have polar bonds but the molecule is nonpolar overall?
workedExamples.solution: C-O: electronegativity difference = 3.5 - 2.5 = 1.0. This is polar covalent (between 0.4 and 1.7). C-H: difference = 2.5 - 2.1 = 0.4, which is considered nonpolar covalent (≤ 0.4). In CO2, each C=O bond is polar, but the molecule is linear (O=C=O), so the two bond dipoles point in opposite directions and cancel out. The molecule has no net dipole moment.
workedExamples.explanation: Electronegativity differences determine bond type, but molecular polarity depends on BOTH bond polarity AND molecular shape. Symmetric molecules like CO2 cancel their bond dipoles, while asymmetric molecules like H₂O retain a net dipole. This distinction is crucial for understanding solubility, boiling points, and intermolecular forces.

commonMistakes: Confusing atomic number and mass number — atomic number is protons (bottom on periodic table), mass number is protons + neutrons.
Thinking isotopes have different chemical properties — isotopes differ in neutrons, which barely affect chemistry. Chemical properties depend on electrons.
Not knowing the difference between ionic and covalent — ionic = electron transfer (metal + nonmetal), covalent = electron sharing (nonmetal + nonmetal).

quiz.question: How many protons does Oxygen (atomic number 8) have?
quiz.explanation: The atomic number tells you the number of protons. Oxygen has atomic number 8, so it has 8 protons.

quiz.question: What type of bond forms between a metal and a nonmetal?
quiz.explanation: Ionic bonds form when a metal transfers electrons to a nonmetal, creating oppositely charged ions that attract each other.

quiz.question: How many valence electrons does Carbon (atomic number 6) have?
quiz.explanation: Carbon has electron configuration 2, 4. The outer shell (valence) has 4 electrons, which is why carbon can form 4 bonds.

quiz.question: What is the atomic number of an element?
options: The total number of protons and neutrons
The number of protons in the nucleus
The number of electrons in the outer shell
The mass of the atom in atomic mass units
quiz.explanation: The atomic number equals the number of protons in the nucleus. It defines the element: all atoms with 6 protons are carbon, regardless of neutrons or electrons.

nodes.name: Chemical Reactions
nodes.description: Balancing equations, stoichiometry, and reaction types.
keyConcepts: Types of reactions (synthesis, decomposition, single/double replacement, combustion)
nodes.whyItMatters: Chemical reactions power your body (metabolism), your car (combustion), and the planet (photosynthesis). Stoichiometry lets you calculate exactly how much reactant you need.
practiceProblems: Balance: H₂ + O₂ → H₂O
How many grams of CO₂ from 44g of C₃H₈ combustion?
Identify type: 2Na + Cl₂ → 2NaCl
Limiting reactant: 2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O with 4mol H₂ and 1mol O₂

resources.title: Khan Academy: Chemical Reactions
resources.whyHelpful: Khan Academy unit on balancing equations, stoichiometry, and reaction types. Structured exercises help build confidence with mole calculations.

resources.title: Balancing Equations Tutorial
resources.whyHelpful: Step-by-step video walkthrough of balancing chemical equations. Watch this if you are stuck on the mechanics of balancing.

resources.title: Crash Course Chemistry: Chemical Reactions
resources.whyHelpful: Entertaining overview of reaction types, balancing, and stoichiometry. Good for a high-level understanding before diving into details.

resources.title: ChemCollective: Virtual Labs
resources.whyHelpful: Simulated chemistry lab — mix reagents, solve stoichiometry problems, and practice limiting reactant calculations without real chemicals.

resources.title: PhET: Balancing Chemical Equations
resources.whyHelpful: Interactive simulation that builds stoichiometry intuition. Drag coefficients to balance equations with real-time visual feedback.

workedExamples.problem: Balance the equation: H₂ + O₂ → H₂O. Then identify the limiting reactant if you have 4 mol H₂ and 1 mol O₂.
workedExamples.solution: Balanced: 2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O. The ratio is 2:1:2. For 4 mol H₂, you need 2 mol O₂. You only have 1 mol O₂, so O₂ is limiting. It produces 2 mol H₂O. H₂ is in excess (2 mol left over).
workedExamples.explanation: Balancing equations conserves atoms — same number of each element on both sides. The limiting reactant is the one that runs out first and determines the maximum product. Always compare mole ratios from the balanced equation.

workedExamples.problem: Methane (CH₄) burns in oxygen: CH₄ + 2O₂ → CO₂ + 2H₂O. If you burn 16 g of methane (molar mass 16 g/mol), how many grams of CO₂ are produced? How many grams of O₂ are needed?
workedExamples.solution: 16 g CH₄ = 1 mol. From the equation: 1 mol CH₄ produces 1 mol CO₂ (44 g) and requires 2 mol O₂ (64 g). Total mass: 16 + 64 = 80 g reactants = 44 + 36 = 80 g products. Mass is conserved.
workedExamples.explanation: Stoichiometry uses mole ratios from balanced equations to convert between masses. Conservation of mass means total mass of reactants equals total mass of products — atoms are rearranged, not created or destroyed.

workedExamples.problem: 24.3 g of magnesium (24.3 g/mol) reacts with excess hydrochloric acid: Mg + 2HCl → MgCl₂ + H₂. How many liters of H₂ gas are produced at STP (22.4 L/mol)? If you collect 10.0 L, what is the percent yield?
workedExamples.solution: Moles Mg = 24.3/24.3 = 1.00 mol. From the equation: 1 mol Mg produces 1 mol H₂. Volume H₂ = 1.00 × 22.4 = 22.4 L at STP. If actual yield is 10.0 L: percent yield = (10.0/22.4) × 100 = 44.6%. Some gas likely escaped or reacted with air.
workedExamples.explanation: At STP (0°C, 1 atm), 1 mole of any ideal gas occupies 22.4 L (Avogadro's Law). Theoretical yield is the maximum possible from stoichiometry. Actual yield is always lower due to side reactions, gas escape, or incomplete reactions. Percent yield measures reaction efficiency.

commonMistakes: Changing subscripts to balance equations — you can only change coefficients (numbers in front), not subscripts (numbers in formulas).
Forgetting diatomic elements — H₂, O₂, N₂, Cl₂, etc. exist as pairs. Don't write H or O alone in equations.
Using mass instead of moles for limiting reactant — you must convert grams to moles first, then compare mole ratios.

quiz.question: What does the law of conservation of mass state?
options: Mass can be created in reactions
Mass can be destroyed in reactions
Mass is neither created nor destroyed
quiz.explanation: In chemical reactions, mass is conserved. The total mass of reactants equals the total mass of products. This is why equations must be balanced.

quiz.question: In 2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O, if you have 3 mol H₂ and 2 mol O₂, which is the limiting reactant?
quiz.explanation: The ratio is 2 H₂ : 1 O₂. For 3 mol H₂, you need 1.5 mol O₂. You have 2 mol O₂, which is enough. H₂ runs out first, so it is limiting.

quiz.question: Balance this equation: H₂ + O₂ → H₂O. What is the coefficient for H₂?
quiz.explanation: Balanced equation: 2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O. This conserves atoms: 4 H and 2 O on both sides.

quiz.question: What is a limiting reactant in a chemical reaction?
options: The reactant that is present in the smallest amount by mass
The reactant that runs out first and limits how much product can form
The reactant that speeds up the reaction
The reactant that is left over after the reaction
quiz.explanation: The limiting reactant is the one that is completely consumed first, stopping the reaction. The other reactants are in excess. Identifying the limiting reactant is essential for calculating theoretical yield.

nodes.name: Cells & Biology Basics
nodes.description: Cell structure, DNA, and how living things work.
nodes.whyItMatters: Cells are the fundamental units of life. Understanding them means understanding health, disease, genetics, and biotechnology. CRISPR, vaccines, cancer treatment — all start here.
practiceProblems: Name 3 differences between plant and animal cells
What does mitochondria do? (cellular respiration)
Draw DNA base pairing: A pairs with ?, G pairs with ?
Describe the stages of mitosis in order

resources.title: Crash Course Biology
resources.whyHelpful: Hank Green engaging biology video series covering cell structure, DNA, and cell division. Great starting point for visual learners.

resources.title: Khan Academy: Cells
resources.whyHelpful: Khan Academy structured cell biology unit covering organelles, membrane transport, and cell division with built-in quizzes.

resources.title: Inner Body: Cell Anatomy Explorer
resources.whyHelpful: Interactive 3D anatomy explorer for cells. Hover over organelles to learn their function in a visual, hands-on way.

resources.title: The Cell: A Molecular Approach (NCBI Bookshelf)
resources.whyHelpful: Free online textbook covering cell structure, organelles, membrane transport, and the cell cycle at the molecular level. More detailed than Khan Academy.

resources.title: BioInteractive (HHMI)
resources.whyHelpful: Free high-quality biology resources from Howard Hughes Medical Institute. Virtual labs and data-driven investigations on cell biology.

workedExamples.problem: Compare prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells. Name three differences. What does mitochondria do? What are the base pairing rules in DNA?
workedExamples.solution: Prokaryotes: no nucleus, no membrane-bound organelles, smaller (bacteria). Eukaryotes: have nucleus, have organelles, larger (plants, animals, fungi). Mitochondria produce ATP (energy) through cellular respiration. DNA pairs: A-T, G-C.
workedExamples.explanation: The nucleus separates DNA from the cytoplasm in eukaryotes. Mitochondria are the powerhouses of the cell. DNA base pairing is specific — adenine only bonds with thymine, guanine only with cytosine. This specificity enables accurate replication.

workedExamples.problem: A protein is being synthesized at a ribosome. Describe the journey from DNA to functional protein. What role does mRNA play? Where does this process occur in prokaryotic vs eukaryotic cells?
workedExamples.solution: 1. DNA is transcribed into mRNA in the nucleus (eukaryotes) or cytoplasm (prokaryotes). 2. mRNA exits the nucleus and binds to ribosomes. 3. Ribosomes read mRNA codons and tRNA brings matching amino acids. 4. Amino acids link to form a polypeptide chain. 5. The protein folds into its functional 3D shape.
workedExamples.explanation: The central dogma of molecular biology: DNA → RNA → Protein. mRNA carries genetic instructions from DNA to ribosomes. In prokaryotes, transcription and translation happen simultaneously in the cytoplasm since there is no nucleus.

workedExamples.problem: A student looks at a cell under a microscope and sees: a cell wall, chloroplasts, and a large central vacuole. Is this a plant or animal cell? What would you see instead in an animal cell? What process is this cell performing that an animal cell cannot?
workedExamples.solution: Cell wall + chloroplasts + large central vacuole = PLANT CELL. An animal cell would instead have centrioles, lysosomes, and many small vacuoles (no large central one). This plant cell performs photosynthesis (in chloroplasts), which animal cells cannot do. The cell wall provides structural support, allowing plants to grow tall without a skeleton.
workedExamples.explanation: Plant cells have specialized structures for photosynthesis and structural support. Chloroplasts convert light energy to glucose (photosynthesis). The cell wall is made of cellulose. Animal cells lack these because animals eat food for energy rather than making it from sunlight. Understanding cell types is fundamental to biology.

commonMistakes: Thinking all cells look the same — cells are highly specialized. A nerve cell, muscle cell, and red blood cell have very different structures.
Confusing mitosis and meiosis — mitosis produces two identical diploid cells (growth/repair), meiosis produces four genetically different haploid cells (gametes).
Forgetting that viruses are not cells — they cannot reproduce on their own and are not considered living by most biologists.

quiz.question: Which organelle is known as the 'powerhouse of the cell'?
quiz.explanation: Mitochondria generate ATP through cellular respiration, providing the energy that powers most cellular activities.

quiz.question: In DNA, which base pairs with Adenine (A)?
quiz.explanation: In DNA, Adenine pairs with Thymine (A-T), and Guanine pairs with Cytosine (G-C). RNA uses Uracil instead of Thymine.

quiz.question: What are the three parts of the cell theory?
options: Cells are small, cells divide, cells have DNA
All living things are made of cells, cells are basic units of life, all cells come from existing cells
Cells have nuclei, cells make energy, cells reproduce
Cells are prokaryotic, cells are eukaryotic, cells are viral
quiz.explanation: Cell theory states: 1) All living organisms are composed of one or more cells, 2) The cell is the basic unit of life, 3) All cells arise from existing cells.

quiz.question: What is the function of the cell membrane?
options: To produce energy for the cell
To control what enters and exits the cell
quiz.explanation: The cell membrane is a selectively permeable barrier that controls the movement of substances in and out of the cell. It maintains homeostasis by allowing nutrients in and waste products out.

nodes.name: Evolution & Genetics
nodes.description: Natural selection, inheritance, and the history of life.
nodes.whyItMatters: Evolution is the unifying theory of biology. It explains antibiotic resistance, dog breeding, and why you exist. Genetics explains inheritance, disease risk, and ancestry.
practiceProblems: Explain how antibiotic resistance evolves in bacteria
Punnett square: Aa × Aa. What are offspring probabilities?
What is a mutation and how can it be beneficial?
Define: genotype, phenotype, allele, gene

resources.title: Khan Academy: Evolution
resources.whyHelpful: Khan Academy evolution unit covering natural selection, adaptation, and speciation. Structured lessons with practice questions.

resources.title: Khan Academy: Genetics
resources.whyHelpful: Khan Academy genetics unit covering Mendelian inheritance, Punnett squares, and DNA replication. Essential companion to the evolution material.

resources.title: Understanding Evolution (UC Berkeley)
resources.whyHelpful: UC Berkeley definitive evolution education site with clear explanations, case studies, and interactive content for all levels.

resources.title: Crash Course: Evolution & Natural Selection
resources.whyHelpful: Hank Green makes evolution entertaining and visual — covers natural selection, speciation, and Hardy-Weinberg with animations that make abstract concepts concrete.

resources.title: Your Genome (Wellcome Trust)
resources.whyHelpful: Clear, accessible guide to DNA, genes, and the human genome project. Interactive features make genetics tangible.

workedExamples.problem: In a population of beetles, some are green and some are brown. Birds preferentially eat green beetles on brown soil. Starting with 50% green and 50% brown, predict what happens over 10 generations. Then do a Punnett square for a cross between two heterozygous parents (Bb × Bb).
workedExamples.solution: Natural selection: brown beetles survive and reproduce more on brown soil. Over 10 generations, the brown allele frequency increases. Punnett square Bb × Bb: 25% BB, 50% Bb, 25% bb. Phenotypic ratio: 3 brown : 1 green if brown is dominant.
workedExamples.explanation: Natural selection changes allele frequencies over generations when certain traits confer a survival advantage. Mendelian inheritance predicts offspring ratios from parental genotypes. The Punnett square is a simple probability tool.

workedExamples.problem: A population of 1000 individuals has 360 individuals with a recessive phenotype (aa). Using Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium, calculate the frequency of the dominant allele (p) and the expected number of heterozygous carriers.
workedExamples.solution: q² = 360/1000 = 0.36, so q = 0.6. p = 1 - 0.6 = 0.4. Heterozygous frequency = 2pq = 2(0.4)(0.6) = 0.48. Expected carriers = 0.48 × 1000 = 480 individuals.
workedExamples.explanation: Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium (p² + 2pq + q² = 1) lets us calculate allele and genotype frequencies in a population. This is foundational for understanding genetic diversity, disease carrier rates, and how populations evolve.

workedExamples.problem: A mutation gives 10% of a moth population white wings (recessive trait). After an industrial revolution, soot covers the trees, making white moths more visible to predators. White moth survival rate drops to 50% of black moths. After one generation of selection, what happens to the allele frequency? How does this relate to the peppered moth case?
workedExamples.solution: Let q² = 0.10 (white moths). So q = sqrt(0.10) = 0.316. p = 0.684. After selection, white moths (aa) have 50% survival. The new frequency of a alleles decreases because fewer aa individuals survive to reproduce. This is directional selection. In the real peppered moth case in England, dark moths went from rare to dominant on soot-covered trees — then reversed when air quality improved. This is one of the clearest documented cases of evolution in action.
workedExamples.explanation: Natural selection doesn't need to kill every individual with an unfavorable trait — even a modest survival advantage accumulates across generations. Industrial melanism is a real example: before industrialization, light moths were camouflaged on lichen-covered trees; after soot darkened the trees, dark moths had the advantage. Evolution happens to populations, not individuals.

commonMistakes: Confusing correlation with causation — just because two things happen together doesn't mean one causes the other. Ice cream sales and drowning deaths both rise in summer, but ice cream doesn't cause drowning.
Accepting a single study as definitive — science advances through replication. One study is a data point, not a conclusion.
Ignoring sample size and methodology — a study with 5 people is vastly weaker than one with 5,000.

quiz.question: What is the correct order of the scientific method?
quiz.explanation: The scientific method starts with observation, then forms a hypothesis, tests it with experiments, and draws a conclusion based on the results.

quiz.question: Why is peer review important in science?
options: Other experts check the work for errors and biases
It guarantees the results are true
It allows scientists to copy each other
quiz.explanation: Peer review means independent experts evaluate a study before publication. It catches errors, biases, and flawed methodology, but it doesn't guarantee truth.

quiz.question: What is the difference between correlation and causation?
options: Correlation means one causes the other; causation is just a relationship
Correlation is a relationship; causation means one directly causes the other
quiz.explanation: Correlation means two variables change together. Causation means one variable directly causes the change in the other. Ice cream sales and drowning are correlated (both rise in summer) but neither causes the other.

quiz.question: What is natural selection?
options: The process by which organisms choose their traits
The process by which organisms with traits better suited to their environment tend to survive and reproduce
The random mutation of genes over time
The breeding of organisms by humans for desired traits
quiz.explanation: Natural selection is the mechanism of evolution where organisms with advantageous traits are more likely to survive and pass those traits to offspring. Over generations, this leads to adaptation and speciation.

nodes.name: Electricity & Magnetism
nodes.description: Voltage, current, resistance, circuits, and magnetic fields. The physics that powers modern civilization.
nodes.whyItMatters: Electricity powers everything — your computer, phone, lights, and the internet. Understanding circuits, voltage, and magnetic fields helps you debug electronics, grasp battery specs, and appreciate how the grid delivers power to your home.
practiceProblems: A circuit has a 9V battery and a 3Ω resistor. What is the current?
Two 10Ω resistors in parallel: what is the total resistance?
A 60W lightbulb runs on 120V. What current does it draw?
Explain how a transformer changes voltage using electromagnetic induction

resources.title: Crash Course: Electricity
resources.whyHelpful: 20-minute crash course covering voltage, current, resistance, and circuits with real-world demonstrations. Great starting point.

resources.title: Khan Academy: Circuits
resources.whyHelpful: Interactive circuit lessons from basic Ohm's Law through complex circuit analysis. Includes simulation exercises.

resources.title: PhET Circuit Construction Kit
resources.whyHelpful: Build virtual circuits with batteries, resistors, bulbs, and switches. See voltage and current in real time.

resources.title: Veritasium: How Electricity Actually Works
resources.whyHelpful: Mind-bending explanation of what electric current actually IS (spoiler: electrons move slowly, but the energy travels at light speed).

quiz.question: According to Ohm's Law, what is the current through a 12V battery connected to a 4Ω resistor?
quiz.explanation: I = V/R = 12/4 = 3 A. Ohm's Law is the fundamental relationship between voltage, current, and resistance.

quiz.question: Two 6Ω resistors are connected in parallel. What is the total resistance?
quiz.explanation: For parallel resistors: 1/R_total = 1/R1 + 1/R2 = 1/6 + 1/6 = 1/3. R_total = 3Ω. Parallel paths decrease total resistance.

quiz.question: A 100W lightbulb runs on 120V. What current flows through it?
quiz.explanation: P = IV, so I = P/V = 100/120 = 0.83 A. Power equals current times voltage.

workedExamples.problem: A circuit has three resistors: R1=10Ω, R2=20Ω in series, and R3=30Ω in parallel with the series pair. The battery is 12V. Find the total resistance, total current, and power output.
workedExamples.solution: Step 1: R1+R2 series = 10+20 = 30Ω. Step 2: Series pair in parallel with R3: 1/R_total = 1/30 + 1/30 = 2/30 → R_total = 15Ω. Step 3: Total current I = V/R = 12/15 = 0.8 A. Step 4: Power P = I²R = 0.64×15 = 9.6 W. Or P = IV = 0.8×12 = 9.6 W.
workedExamples.explanation: Series resistors add directly. Parallel resistors reduce total resistance. The power formula P = IV (or P = I²R) gives total power dissipated across the circuit.

workedExamples.problem: A house has a 240V mains supply. Four appliances are connected in parallel: a 2000W kettle, 1000W microwave, 100W light, and 500W refrigerator. What current does each draw? What is the total current? Will this trip a 20A breaker?
workedExamples.solution: Kettle: I=P/V=2000/240=8.33A. Microwave: 1000/240=4.17A. Light: 100/240=0.42A. Refrigerator: 500/240=2.08A. Total I = 8.33+4.17+0.42+2.08 = 15A. The 20A breaker will NOT trip (15A < 20A), but adding one more high-power appliance could overload it.
workedExamples.explanation: Parallel circuits split current across branches, but the total current must not exceed the circuit breaker rating. This is why turning on too many appliances trips the breaker.

workedExamples.problem: A step-down transformer has 500 turns on the primary coil and 50 turns on the secondary. The primary is connected to 240V. What is the secondary voltage? If the secondary current is 10A, what is the primary current (assuming ideal transformer)?
workedExamples.solution: V_s/V_p = N_s/N_p → V_s/240 = 50/500 = 0.1 → V_s = 24V. For an ideal transformer: V_p × I_p = V_s × I_s → 240 × I_p = 24 × 10 → I_p = 240/240 = 1 A.
workedExamples.explanation: Transformers use electromagnetic induction to change voltage. Step-down transformers reduce voltage but increase current proportionally (conserving power). This is how your phone charger converts 240V wall power to 5V USB power.

commonMistakes: Confusing series and parallel — in series, current is the same through all components; in parallel, voltage is the same across all branches.
Forgetting that household wiring is parallel, not series — each outlet gets the same 120/240V regardless of what's plugged into others.
Thinking electric current is like water flow (simple analogy, but inaccurate — electrons drift at mm/s while the electric field travels at lightspeed).

nodes.name: Special Relativity
nodes.description: Einstein's theory of special relativity redefined our understanding of space, time, and energy. Discover how time slows down at high speeds, how length contracts, and how the most famous equation in physics — E=mc² — reveals that mass and energy are interchangeable.
keyConcepts: Principle of relativity: the laws of physics are the same in all inertial reference frames
Speed of light is constant in all frames (c = 3×10⁸ m/s)
Time dilation: moving clocks run slower relative to stationary observers
Length contraction: moving objects appear shorter in the direction of motion
Lorentz transformations: the math that relates space and time between frames
Relativistic momentum and energy: E = mc² and why nothing can reach light speed
Twin paradox: why one twin ages less after a high-speed space trip
nodes.whyItMatters: Special relativity is essential for understanding GPS satellites (which experience time dilation), particle accelerators (where particles approach light speed), and nuclear energy (E=mc²). Without relativity corrections, GPS would drift by 10km per day. It's not abstract — it's engineered into every phone with location services.
practiceProblems: A spaceship travels at 0.8c relative to Earth. If the crew measures 10 years on the ship, how much time passes on Earth? (Use the Lorentz factor γ = 1/√(1-v²/c²))
A muon particle has a half-life of 2.2µs at rest. If it's moving at 0.99c, how far can it travel before decaying? (Calculate using both classical and relativistic approaches)
1 gram of matter is completely converted to energy via E=mc². How much energy is released? Express in joules and compare to the Hiroshima atomic bomb (~6×10¹³ J).

Two spacecraft pass each other, each traveling at 0.6c relative to Earth but in opposite directions. According to special relativity, what is the relative speed between the two spacecraft? (Hint: it's not 1.2c — use the relativistic velocity addition formula.)

resources.title: 3Blue1Brown: Relativity Visualized
resources.whyHelpful: 3Blue1Brown's visual explanations of special relativity — spacetime diagrams, Lorentz transformations, and the twin paradox with intuitive geometric reasoning.

resources.title: Khan Academy: Special Relativity
resources.whyHelpful: Khan Academy's complete course on special relativity — time dilation, length contraction, relativistic momentum, and E=mc² with clear step-by-step explanations.

resources.title: MinutePhysics: Special Relativity Playlist
resources.whyHelpful: Henry Reich's engaging short animated videos explaining relativity concepts through simple thought experiments and everyday analogies.

resources.title: E=mc²: Einstein's Big Idea (NOVA)
resources.whyHelpful: NOVA documentary on the most famous equation — how E=mc² emerged from relativity, its proof in nuclear physics, and its role in modern technology from PET scans to nuclear power.

quiz.question: What does the constancy of the speed of light imply?
options: Light travels at different speeds for different observers
All observers measure the speed of light as 3×10⁸ m/s, regardless of their motion
Light speed increases when the source moves toward you
Only stationary observers can measure light's speed
quiz.explanation: A key postulate of special relativity is that the speed of light in vacuum is the same for all inertial observers, regardless of the source's or observer's motion. This contradicts everyday experience (where velocities add), and it leads to the strange consequences of time dilation and length contraction.

quiz.question: What is time dilation?
options: Time passes slower in strong gravity
A moving clock ticks slower relative to a stationary observer
Time only exists in the present moment
Time speeds up as you age
quiz.explanation: Time dilation means that a clock moving relative to a stationary observer ticks slower. The effect is described by Δt = γ·Δt₀, where γ = 1/√(1-v²/c²) is the Lorentz factor. At everyday speeds the effect is negligible, but at 99.5% of light speed, γ ≈ 10 — time passes 10× slower for the moving object.

quiz.question: What does E=mc² mean?
options: Energy equals mass times the speed of light squared, meaning mass can be converted to enormous energy
Energy and mass are the same thing
The speed of light determines how much energy matter has
quiz.explanation: E=mc² states that mass (m) is a form of energy (E), with the conversion factor being c² (speed of light squared). Since c is 3×10⁸ m/s, c² is an enormous number: 9×10¹⁶. This means even a tiny mass contains staggering energy — 1 kg of matter converted completely releases 9×10¹⁶ J, equivalent to about 21 megatons of TNT.

workedExamples.problem: A spaceship travels at v = 0.9c relative to Earth. (a) Calculate the Lorentz factor γ. (b) If 5 years pass on Earth, how much time passes on the ship? (c) If the ship measures a 100m-long object at rest, how long would an Earth observer measure it?
workedExamples.solution: (a) γ = 1/√(1 - v²/c²) = 1/√(1 - 0.9²) = 1/√(1 - 0.81) = 1/√0.19 = 1/0.4359 ≈ 2.294. (b) Earth time Δt = 5 years, ship proper time Δt₀ = Δt/γ = 5/2.294 ≈ 2.18 years. Only 2.18 years pass on the ship while 5 years pass on Earth. (c) The ship measures proper length L₀ = 100m. Earth observer measures contracted length L = L₀/γ = 100/2.294 ≈ 43.6m. The spaceship and everything on it appears squished to 44% of its rest length when viewed from Earth.
workedExamples.explanation: This example shows the symmetry of relativity: from Earth's perspective, the ship experiences time dilation and length contraction. But from the ship's perspective, Earth is the one moving and experiences the same effects. This symmetry is only broken when one frame accelerates (as in the twin paradox), which resolves the apparent contradiction.

workedExamples.problem: How much energy is released when 0.5 grams of matter is completely converted to energy? Express in joules and in equivalent TNT tons (1 ton TNT = 4.184×10⁹ J). How does this compare to the Hiroshima atomic bomb (~15 kilotons)? (c = 3×10⁸ m/s)
workedExamples.solution: E = mc² = (0.0005 kg) × (3×10⁸ m/s)² = 0.0005 × 9×10¹⁶ = 4.5×10¹³ J. In TNT tons: 4.5×10¹³ / 4.184×10⁹ = 10,753 tons ≈ 10.75 kilotons of TNT. Comparison: Hiroshima was ~15 kilotons. So converting just half a gram of mass to energy yields about 72% of the energy of the Hiroshima bomb! This illustrates why nuclear reactions are millions of times more energetic than chemical reactions — a chemical explosion converts essentially no mass to energy, while nuclear reactions convert a tiny fraction.
workedExamples.explanation: E=mc² explains why nuclear power is so dense. A nuclear reactor's fuel rod lasts years on a few kilograms of uranium, while a coal plant burns trainloads daily. In nuclear fission, about 0.1% of the fuel's mass converts to energy — still tiny, but the c² factor makes it enormous. If we could achieve 100% mass-to-energy conversion (matter-antimatter annihilation), a single gram would power a city for a year.

workedExamples.problem: Two rockets approach Earth from opposite directions, each traveling at 0.8c relative to Earth. (a) What is their relative speed according to classical (Galilean) relativity? (b) What is their relative speed according to special relativity? (c) Explain which is correct and why.
workedExamples.solution: (a) Classical: v_rel = v₁ + v₂ = 0.8c + 0.8c = 1.6c — which exceeds the speed of light. (b) Relativistic velocity addition: u' = (u + v)/(1 + uv/c²). From rocket A's perspective, Earth moves at 0.8c to the left, and rocket B moves relative to Earth at 0.8c to the left. So u' = (0.8c + 0.8c)/(1 + 0.8×0.8) = (1.6c)/(1 + 0.64) = 1.6c/1.64 = 0.976c. (c) Special relativity is correct. The relativistic formula ensures no relative speed ever exceeds c. Even if both rockets traveled at 0.999c, their relative speed would be (0.999c + 0.999c)/(1 + 0.999²) ≈ 0.9999995c — close to but not exceeding c.
workedExamples.explanation: This example demonstrates a fundamental principle: nothing can exceed the speed of light, not even relative velocities. The classical addition formula v = v₁ + v₂ only works at everyday speeds (v << c). At relativistic speeds, you must use the relativistic velocity addition formula, which ensures the result is always less than c. This is why two particles approaching from opposite directions at 99% of light speed don't see each other at 198% of light speed.

commonMistakes: Thinking time dilation is caused by mechanical effects on clocks (like vibration or gravity) — it's actually a fundamental property of spacetime itself. Time literally passes at different rates for different observers.

Believing E=mc² means 'everything is energy' or that mass turns into energy in chemical reactions — in chemical reactions, mass is conserved to extremely high precision. E=mc² applies to nuclear reactions and matter-antimatter annihilation where measurable mass converts to energy.
Thinking you can just add relativistic velocities like everyday speeds (v₁ + v₂) — this only works at v << c. Always use the relativistic addition formula: u' = (u+v)/(1+uv/c²).

nodes.name: Organic Chemistry
nodes.description: Organic chemistry is the study of carbon-based compounds — the chemistry of life itself. Learn how carbon's unique bonding creates an infinite variety of molecules, from simple hydrocarbons to complex proteins and DNA. Discover functional groups, polymers, and how organic chemistry explains everything from plastics to pharmaceuticals to the taste of food.
keyConcepts: Carbon's tetravalency: four bonds create chain, branch, and ring structures
Hydrocarbons: alkanes, alkenes, alkynes, and aromatic compounds
Functional groups: alcohols, carboxylic acids, amines, esters, and more
Isomerism: same formula, different structures (structural and stereoisomers)
Polymers: long chains of repeating monomers (plastics, proteins, DNA)
Biomolecules: carbohydrates, lipids, proteins, and nucleic acids
Reaction types: addition, substitution, elimination, and polymerization
nodes.whyItMatters: Organic chemistry is the language of biology and the foundation of modern medicine, materials science, and biochemistry. Every pharmaceutical drug, every plastic, every synthetic fabric, every pesticide, and every flavor molecule is an organic compound. Understanding organic chemistry helps you understand how medications work, why Teflon is non-stick, how DNA stores information, and what 'organic' actually means in food.
practiceProblems: Draw and name all structural isomers of pentane (C₅H₁₂). How many are there?
Identify the functional groups in aspirin (acetylsalicylic acid): it has a carboxylic acid group (-COOH) and an ester group (-COO-). Explain how each contributes to aspirin's properties.
Ethanol (C₂H₅OH) boils at 78°C while ethane (C₂H₆) boils at -89°C. Both have similar molecular weight. Explain why ethanol's boiling point is so much higher.
Nylon-6,6 is made from hexamethylenediamine (6 carbons) and adipic acid (6 carbons). What type of polymerization reaction is this (addition or condensation)? How does the polymer chain form?

resources.title: Khan Academy: Organic Chemistry
resources.whyHelpful: Khan Academy's complete organic chemistry course — from basic hydrocarbons to complex reaction mechanisms with clear video explanations and practice problems.

resources.title: Crash Course Organic Chemistry
resources.whyHelpful: Hank Green's engaging 50-episode series covering everything from functional groups to organic synthesis to biochemistry applications.

resources.title: Organic Chemistry as a Second Language
resources.whyHelpful: David Klein's renowned textbook that breaks down organic chemistry into manageable concepts. Known for its intuitive approach to reaction mechanisms and resonance structures.

resources.title: Biomolecules (Khan Academy)
resources.whyHelpful: Khan Academy's section on biological macromolecules — how organic chemistry principles explain carbohydrates, proteins, lipids, and nucleic acids in living systems.

quiz.question: What makes carbon uniquely suited for forming complex molecules?
options: It can form four stable covalent bonds, allowing chain, branch, and ring structures
It's the lightest element that can form bonds
quiz.explanation: Carbon has four valence electrons and can form four stable covalent bonds. This tetravalency allows carbon atoms to bond with each other in chains, branches, and rings of almost unlimited complexity. Combined with the ability to form single, double, and triple bonds, carbon can create millions of distinct compounds — more than all other elements combined.

quiz.question: What are functional groups in organic chemistry?
options: Groups of atoms that react the same way in medical tests
Specific arrangements of atoms that give molecules their characteristic chemical properties and reactivity
Groups that prevent molecules from reacting
Atoms that don't participate in chemical reactions
quiz.explanation: Functional groups are specific groups of atoms within molecules that are responsible for characteristic chemical reactions. Examples include hydroxyl (-OH, alcohols), carboxyl (-COOH, acids), amino (-NH₂, amines), and carbonyl (C=O, aldehydes/ketones). The same functional group behaves similarly in different molecules, which is why organic chemistry is systematic rather than memorizing millions of individual reactions.

quiz.question: What are isomers?
options: Molecules with different formulas but similar structures
Molecules with the same molecular formula but different arrangements of atoms
quiz.explanation: Isomers are compounds with the same molecular formula but different structural arrangements. Structural isomers differ in how atoms are connected (e.g., butane vs isobutane, both C₄H₁₀). Stereoisomers have the same connections but different spatial arrangements (e.g., cis vs trans, or left-handed vs right-handed mirror-image molecules). Drug chirality matters — one isomer of thalidomide was therapeutic, the other caused birth defects.

workedExamples.problem: Identify and name all structural isomers of hexane (C₆H₁₄). How many non-cyclic isomers exist? Draw their carbon skeletons.
workedExamples.solution: Hexane has 5 structural isomers. (1) n-hexane: a straight 6-carbon chain (C-C-C-C-C-C). (2) 2-methylpentane: a 5-carbon chain with a methyl branch on carbon #2 (C-C(C)-C-C-C). (3) 3-methylpentane: a 5-carbon chain with a methyl branch on carbon #3 (C-C-C(C)-C-C). (4) 2,2-dimethylbutane: a 4-carbon chain with two methyl groups on carbon #2 (C-C(C)(C)-C). (5) 2,3-dimethylbutane: a 4-carbon chain with methyl groups on carbons #2 and #3 (C-C(C)-C(C)-C). Isomerism explains why C₆H₁₄ corresponds to 5 distinct substances with different boiling points, densities, and physical properties.
workedExamples.explanation: Structural isomerism arises because carbon atoms can arrange themselves in different connectivity patterns even with the same formula. As carbon chains get longer, the number of possible isomers explodes: C₁₀H₂₂ has 75 isomers, C₂₀H₄₂ has 366,319, and C₄₀H₈₂ has 62,491,178,805,831. This combinatorial explosion is why organic chemistry can produce millions of distinct molecules from just a few elements.

workedExamples.problem: Compare the boiling points of ethanol (C₂H₅OH, MW=46), dimethyl ether (CH₃OCH₃, MW=46), and propane (C₃H₈, MW=44). All three have similar molecular weights. Their boiling points are: ethanol 78°C, dimethyl ether -24°C, propane -42°C. Explain the dramatic differences.
workedExamples.solution: Despite similar molecular weights, the intermolecular forces differ drastically: (1) Ethanol has an -OH (hydroxyl) group capable of strong hydrogen bonding (O-H···O). Hydrogen bonds require ~20 kJ/mol to break, compared to ~2 kJ/mol for van der Waals forces. This explains ethanol's high 78°C boiling point. (2) Dimethyl ether has an oxygen atom but no O-H bond — it can only accept hydrogen bonds (weak dipole-dipole interactions), giving a much lower -24°C boiling point. (3) Propane is nonpolar — only weak London dispersion forces hold the molecules together, resulting in the lowest boiling point at -42°C. This example shows how functional groups dominate physical properties.
workedExamples.explanation: Functional groups determine not just chemical reactivity but physical properties like boiling point, solubility, and state of matter. The hydroxyl group (-OH) in alcohols creates hydrogen bonding, making them water-soluble and high-boiling. Hydrocarbons without functional groups are nonpolar and water-insoluble. This is why oil and water don't mix — water molecules hydrogen-bond with each other, excluding nonpolar oil molecules.

workedExamples.problem: Polyethylene is the world's most common plastic, with over 100 million tons produced annually. (a) What monomer is used to make polyethylene? (b) What type of polymerization reaction forms polyethylene? (c) How does the difference between HDPE (high-density) and LDPE (low-density) polyethylene relate to the polymer structure?
workedExamples.solution: (a) Polyethylene is made from ethylene (ethene) monomers — C₂H₄, a 2-carbon molecule with a double bond (CH₂=CH₂). (b) Addition polymerization: the double bond opens up, allowing thousands of ethylene molecules to join in a chain — (CH₂-CH₂)ₙ. No byproducts are formed (unlike condensation polymerization which releases water). (c) HDPE has mostly linear polymer chains with minimal branching — the chains pack tightly together, forming crystalline regions that make HDPE rigid and strong (used for milk jugs, piping). LDPE has significant branching during polymerization — the chains can't pack as tightly, resulting in lower density, more flexibility, and transparency (used for plastic bags, squeeze bottles). The catalyst controls the branching level.
workedExamples.explanation: Polymers demonstrate how simple monomers can create materials with vastly different properties through control of chain structure. The difference between a milk jug and a plastic bag is just the amount of branching in the polymer chains. This structure-property relationship is the central concept of materials science — by controlling molecular architecture, we can design materials with specific mechanical, thermal, and optical properties.

commonMistakes: Thinking 'organic' in chemistry means the same as 'organic' in food — in chemistry, 'organic' means carbon-based (including synthetic compounds like plastics). In food, 'organic' means grown without synthetic pesticides (which are also organic compounds!). They're different concepts using the same word.

Assuming longer carbon chains always mean higher boiling points — while generally true for straight chains, branching reduces boiling points. 2,2-dimethylpropane (C₅H₁₂, bp 9.5°C) boils 27°C lower than n-pentane (bp 36°C) despite having the same formula.

Believing that all organic compounds come from living things — many are synthesized industrially from petroleum. Nylon, Teflon, Kevlar, and most modern plastics are entirely synthetic organic compounds that never existed in nature.

nodes.name: Astrophysics & Cosmology
nodes.description: Journey from the smallest subatomic particles to the largest structures in the universe. Explore how stars are born and die, what black holes really are, the evidence for the Big Bang, the mysteries of dark matter and dark energy, and our place in the cosmos.
keyConcepts: Stellar evolution: from protostars to white dwarfs, neutron stars, and black holes
Nuclear fusion in stars: how stars generate energy (pp chain, CNO cycle)
Hertzsprung-Russell diagram: classifying stars by luminosity and temperature
Black holes: event horizon, singularity, and gravitational waves
Galaxies: spiral, elliptical, irregular — and supermassive black holes at their centers
The Big Bang and cosmic microwave background (CMB) radiation
Dark matter (27% of universe) and dark energy (68%) — what we don't know
Expanding universe and Hubble's law: redshift tells us the universe is accelerating
nodes.whyItMatters: Astrophysics connects everything you've learned — nuclear physics (how stars burn), relativity (black holes, expanding universe), quantum mechanics (neutron stars), and electromagnetism (how we observe distant galaxies). It addresses the biggest questions: Where did the universe come from? Are we alone? What is the ultimate fate of the cosmos? Understanding these questions develops scientific humility and wonder.
practiceProblems: A star has 10 times the mass of the Sun. Use the mass-luminosity relation (L ∝ M³·⁵) to estimate its luminosity relative to the Sun. How does this affect its lifetime? (The Sun's lifetime is ~10 billion years.)

The Andromeda galaxy is about 2.5 million light-years away. If it contains ~1 trillion stars, estimate the average distance between stars in Andromeda. Compare to the average distance between stars in the Milky Way's neighborhood (~4 light-years).

A star's spectrum shows a hydrogen-alpha line at 658nm instead of its rest wavelength of 656.3nm. What is the star's radial velocity? Is it moving toward or away from us? (Use the Doppler formula: v/c = Δλ/λ₀)
The Hubble constant H₀ ≈ 70 km/s/Mpc. If a galaxy is 100 Mpc away, how fast is it receding? How long would it take for that galaxy's distance to double (the Hubble time)?

resources.title: PBS Space Time
resources.whyHelpful: The best astrophysics YouTube channel — covers black holes, quantum gravity, the Big Bang, and dark matter with real physics and math, not oversimplification.

resources.title: NASA: Astronomy & Astrophysics
resources.whyHelpful: NASA's official astrophysics portal with the latest discoveries from Hubble, James Webb, Chandra, and other space observatories.

resources.title: Khan Academy: Cosmology and Astronomy
resources.whyHelpful: Khan Academy's comprehensive astronomy course covering stellar evolution, galaxy classification, the Big Bang, and the large-scale structure of the universe.

resources.title: James Webb Space Telescope (NASA)
resources.whyHelpful: NASA's official James Webb Space Telescope portal with the latest deep-field images, exoplanet discoveries, and early-universe observations. A living example of astrophysics in action.

quiz.question: What determines the final stage of a star's life (white dwarf, neutron star, or black hole)?
quiz.explanation: A star's initial mass determines its entire evolutionary path. Low-mass stars (<8 solar masses) become white dwarfs. Medium-mass stars (8-20 solar masses) become neutron stars after a supernova. High-mass stars (>20 solar masses) become black holes. The more massive the star, the shorter its life and the more violent its death.

quiz.question: What is the cosmic microwave background (CMB) radiation?
options: The leftover heat from the Big Bang, now cooled to 2.7 Kelvin
Radiation from all the stars in the universe
quiz.explanation: The CMB is the oldest light in the universe — a 'snapshot' of the universe when it was only 380,000 years old (the first time it became transparent). Initially a blazing ~3000K, the expansion of the universe has stretched (redshifted) this light to microwave wavelengths, corresponding to a temperature of just 2.725K. It was discovered accidentally in 1965 and is the strongest evidence for the Big Bang.

quiz.question: What are dark matter and dark energy?
options: Dark matter is antimatter, dark energy is vacuum energy
Dark matter explains why galaxies rotate faster than visible mass predicts; dark energy explains why the universe's expansion is accelerating
They are the same thing with different names
Dark matter is made of black holes, dark energy is made of radiation
quiz.explanation: Dark matter (27% of the universe) is an invisible form of matter that interacts gravitationally but doesn't emit or absorb light — it explains why galaxies rotate faster than their visible mass would allow. Dark energy (68%) is a mysterious force causing the universe's expansion to accelerate. Together, they make up 95% of the universe, yet we don't know what either is made of — one of science's biggest open questions.

workedExamples.problem: A star's spectrum shows that its hydrogen-alpha absorption line is shifted from 656.28 nm (rest wavelength) to 656.50 nm. (a) Is the star moving toward or away from us? (b) What is its radial velocity? (c) If this star is in a galaxy 50 Mpc away, what would Hubble's law predict for its recessional velocity? (c = 3×10⁵ km/s, H₀ = 70 km/s/Mpc)
workedExamples.solution: (a) The wavelength is longer (redshifted), meaning the star is moving away from us. (b) Using the Doppler formula: v = c × Δλ/λ₀ = (3×10⁵) × (656.50 - 656.28)/656.28 = 3×10⁵ × 0.22/656.28 = 3×10⁵ × 0.000335 = 100.5 km/s away from us. (c) Hubble's law: v = H₀ × d = 70 × 50 = 3,500 km/s. The actual measured velocity (100 km/s) is much smaller than the Hubble flow velocity (3,500 km/s), because most of the star's motion is due to local gravitational interactions within its galaxy, not the universal expansion. Hubble's law applies to the recessional velocity of the galaxy as a whole.
workedExamples.explanation: This example shows how astronomers measure cosmic distances and velocities. Redshift tells us how fast something is moving away. Hubble's law (v = H₀d) tells us that the universe is expanding uniformly — more distant galaxies recede faster. This was Hubble's 1929 discovery that overturned the static universe model and led directly to the Big Bang theory. The constant H₀ ≈ 70 km/s/Mpc means a galaxy 1 Mpc away recedes at 70 km/s.

workedExamples.problem: The Sun has a mass of 2×10³⁰ kg and converts about 4 million tons of mass to energy every second through nuclear fusion. (a) How much energy does the Sun produce per second? (b) At this rate, how long will the Sun's hydrogen fuel last if it can fuse about 10% of its mass? (c = 3×10⁸ m/s)
workedExamples.solution: (a) E = mc² = (4×10⁶ kg) × (3×10⁸ m/s)² = 4×10⁶ × 9×10¹⁶ = 3.6×10²³ J per second. This is equivalent to ~100 billion megatons of TNT exploding every second. (b) Usable hydrogen = 0.10 × 2×10³⁰ kg = 2×10²⁹ kg. But only 0.7% of the hydrogen mass converts to energy in fusion (the rest becomes helium). Usable energy mass = 2×10²⁹ × 0.007 = 1.4×10²⁷ kg. Energy available = 1.4×10²⁷ × (3×10⁸)² = 1.26×10⁴⁴ J. Time = Energy/rate = 1.26×10⁴⁴ / 3.6×10²³ = 3.5×10²⁰ seconds ≈ 11 billion years. The Sun is about 4.6 billion years old, so it has about 6-7 billion years left before becoming a red giant and eventually a white dwarf.
workedExamples.explanation: This calculation shows why stars shine for billions of years despite their enormous energy output. The key insight: while the Sun converts 4 million tons of mass per second, that's a tiny fraction of its total mass (~2×10³⁰ kg). A star like the Sun has enough hydrogen fuel for a 10-billion-year lifetime. This efficient, long-lasting energy production is why stable fusion is the 'holy grail' of clean energy on Earth.

workedExamples.problem: A galaxy at distance d = 100 Mpc is observed to have a recessional velocity of v = 7,000 km/s. (a) What is the Hubble constant implied by this galaxy? (b) Using this H₀ value, estimate the age of the universe (Hubble time). (c) A second galaxy at 200 Mpc is measured at 13,000 km/s — does this confirm or challenge the first measurement? (1 Mpc = 3.086×10¹⁹ km)
workedExamples.solution: (a) H₀ = v/d = 7,000 km/s / 100 Mpc = 70 km/s/Mpc. (b) Hubble time = 1/H₀. First convert H₀ to consistent units: H₀ = 70 km/s/Mpc = 70 / (3.086×10¹⁹ km) = 2.27×10⁻¹⁸ s⁻¹. Age ≈ 1/H₀ = 4.4×10¹⁷ seconds = 13.9 billion years. (c) Galaxy 2: H₀ = 13,000/200 = 65 km/s/Mpc. This is close to 70 — the slight difference is due to peculiar velocities (local gravitational motion) and measurement uncertainty. The consistent H₀ from two different distances confirms the linear Hubble relationship (v ∝ d) and supports the expanding universe model. The currently accepted value is H₀ ≈ 70 km/s/Mpc, giving an age of ~13.8 billion years.
workedExamples.explanation: The Hubble time gives an estimate of the universe's age assuming constant expansion. The actual age of 13.8 billion years is slightly older because the expansion was faster early on (due to gravity slowing it down) and has recently accelerated (due to dark energy). The 2019 Nobel Prize was awarded for measuring this acceleration through Type Ia supernovae — the discovery that the universe's expansion is speeding up, not slowing down.

commonMistakes: Thinking black holes are 'cosmic vacuum cleaners' that suck everything in — they're actually just extremely dense objects. If the Sun were replaced by a black hole of the same mass, Earth would continue orbiting exactly as before. You'd only get pulled in if you crossed the event horizon, which is only ~3 km for a solar-mass black hole.

Believing the Big Bang was an explosion at a single point in space — it was the rapid expansion of space itself everywhere simultaneously. There is no 'center' of the universe because the Big Bang happened everywhere at once.

Confusing 'dark matter' with 'dark energy' — dark matter clumps together and holds galaxies together gravitationally (like an invisible scaffolding), while dark energy is a repulsive force that pushes space apart. They are completely different phenomena named with the unfortunate word 'dark.'

nodes.name: Evolution & Natural Selection
nodes.description: How life changes over time. From Darwin's finches to the molecular machinery of DNA, understand the mechanism that produced every species on Earth — including us.
keyConcepts: Darwin's theory of natural selection: variation, inheritance, differential survival, and adaptation
Evidence for evolution: fossil record, homologous structures, vestigial organs, DNA evidence, observed evolution in real time
Mendelian genetics and how traits are inherited through dominant and recessive alleles
DNA structure and function: the double helix, replication, transcription, translation, and mutations
Speciation: how new species form through geographic isolation, reproductive isolation, and adaptive radiation
Genetic drift, gene flow, and the Hardy-Weinberg principle of population genetics
The tree of life: phylogenetic trees and how we reconstruct evolutionary history
Human evolution: from common ancestors with chimpanzees to Homo sapiens
nodes.whyItMatters: Evolution is the unifying theory of all biology — without it, nothing in life sciences makes sense. Understanding natural selection teaches you how complex systems emerge from simple rules, a pattern that applies far beyond biology (to AI, economics, and even technology). Evolution explains antibiotic resistance (a direct medical threat), the origin of new diseases like COVID-19, and the very structure of your own body and brain. It also develops the most important scientific skill: asking 'why does this exist?' and looking for the functional reason.
practiceProblems: A population of 1000 beetles has 800 green (GG or Gg) and 200 brown (gg). Green is dominant. Calculate the frequency of the g allele using the Hardy-Weinberg equation. Assuming Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium, what percentage will be brown in the next generation?

Two populations of the same bird species are separated by a mountain range. Population A evolves to have shorter, stronger beaks for cracking seeds. Population B evolves longer, curved beaks for drinking nectar. If the mountain range erodes away after 10,000 years, can these birds still interbreed? What concept does this illustrate?

A species of bacteria doubles every 20 minutes. If a single mutation gives resistance to an antibiotic, and that antibiotic is applied, how many resistant bacteria will there be after 24 hours? Use this to explain why overuse of antibiotics is dangerous.

Compare the forelimb bones of a human arm, a whale flipper, a bat wing, and a horse leg. What do the similarities suggest? What do the differences indicate? This is an example of what type of evidence for evolution?

resources.title: Khan Academy: Evolution and Natural Selection
resources.whyHelpful: Khan Academy's comprehensive AP Biology-level coverage of natural selection, population genetics, speciation, and evidence for evolution with interactive exercises.

resources.title: Your Inner Fish (Neil Shubin)
resources.whyHelpful: A PBS documentary series (and book) tracing the evolutionary history of the human body — showing that our arms came from fish fins, our ears from reptile jaws, and our brains from ancient worm-like creatures.

resources.title: Stated Clearly: What is Evolution?
resources.whyHelpful: A clear, well-animated YouTube series that explains evolution from first principles — perfect for building intuition about natural selection without unnecessary jargon.

resources.title: Understanding Evolution (UC Berkeley)
resources.whyHelpful: UC Berkeley's definitive evolution education site with clear explanations of evolutionary mechanisms, evidence, and common misconceptions. The 'Misconceptions about Evolution' section is especially valuable.

quiz.question: What is the fundamental requirement for natural selection to occur?
options: There must be heritable variation in a population
The environment must be changing rapidly
New species must emerge within a single generation
quiz.explanation: Natural selection requires three conditions: (1) variation in traits within a population, (2) these variations are heritable (passed to offspring), and (3) differential survival and reproduction based on those traits. Without heritable variation, evolution cannot occur — even if the environment changes, there's nothing for selection to work on.

quiz.question: Which of the following is the strongest evidence that humans and chimpanzees share a common ancestor?
options: Their DNA sequences are approximately 98-99% identical
quiz.explanation: The 98-99% DNA sequence identity between humans and chimpanzees is the strongest evidence for common ancestry. This genetic similarity extends to many other features: we share vestigial tailbones, have the same bone structure in our hands (homologous structures), and even share endogenous retroviruses integrated at the same locations in our genomes — the ultimate 'fossil' evidence of shared ancestry.

quiz.question: Why do bacteria evolve resistance to antibiotics so quickly, while larger animals rarely develop resistance to predators?
options: Bacteria are smarter than larger animals
Bacteria have much shorter generation times and larger population sizes, so beneficial mutations appear and spread faster
Antibiotics cause mutations that make bacteria resistant
Bacteria intentionally evolve resistance when threatened
quiz.explanation: Bacteria can double in population every 20-30 minutes, meaning thousands of generations can pass in a single year. With billions of bacteria in an infection, even a rare mutation (1 in 10 million) is likely to occur. The few resistant bacteria survive antibiotics and repopulate. This is a powerful real-time demonstration of evolution — we can observe it happening. It's also why completing antibiotic courses is vital: stopping early leaves resistant survivors.

workedExamples.problem: In a population of 500 flowers, 245 are red (RR), 210 are pink (Rr), and 45 are white (rr). (a) Calculate the allele frequencies of R and r. (b) Is this population in Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium? (c) If not, what evolutionary force might be acting?
workedExamples.solution: (a) Total alleles = 500 × 2 = 1,000. R count = 245×2 + 210 = 700. r count = 210 + 45×2 = 300. Frequency of R (p) = 700/1,000 = 0.70. Frequency of r (q) = 300/1,000 = 0.30. (b) Under Hardy-Weinberg, expected: RR = p²×500 = 0.49×500 = 245, Rr = 2pq×500 = 0.42×500 = 210, rr = q²×500 = 0.09×500 = 45. The observed (245, 210, 45) exactly match expected (245, 210, 45). The population IS in Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium for this gene. (c) Since the observed match expected, there is no evidence for selection, mutation, migration, or genetic drift acting on this gene — it is not evolving at this locus.
workedExamples.explanation: The Hardy-Weinberg equation (p² + 2pq + q² = 1) is a null model for evolution: it tells us what genotype frequencies would be if no evolution were occurring. When observed frequencies differ from Hardy-Weinberg predictions, it tells us that some evolutionary force (selection, drift, mutation, or migration) is acting. This example shows a population that IS in equilibrium — no evolution at this particular gene.

workedExamples.problem: A population of 200 birds lives on an island. 50 birds migrate to a nearby island and establish a new population. On the original island, allele A has frequency 0.6. By chance, among the 50 migrants, only 20 carry allele A (frequency 0.4). (a) What effect does this have on allele frequency in the new population? (b) Is this natural selection? (c) How would this differ if a drought killed 90% of the original population?
workedExamples.solution: (a) The new population starts with allele A frequency of 0.4 (from the 20 out of 50 migrants with A). This differs from the original 0.6 due to sampling error — the migrants were not a perfect genetic representation of the source population. This is the founder effect. (b) This is NOT natural selection — no trait provided a survival or reproductive advantage. It's genetic drift caused by a random sampling event. (c) A drought killing 90% of the original population would be a population bottleneck. Survivors might have a different allele frequency from the original population purely by chance (not because they were better adapted to drought). Both founder effects and bottlenecks reduce genetic diversity, making populations more vulnerable to future environmental changes.
workedExamples.explanation: Genetic drift is evolution by chance, not by fitness advantage. The founder effect (small group colonizes new area) and population bottlenecks (catastrophic reduction in population size) are two major forms of drift. Small populations are more subject to drift — chance events can significantly change allele frequencies. This is why endangered species that have gone through bottlenecks (like cheetahs) have very low genetic diversity.

workedExamples.problem: Darwin's finches on the Galápagos island of Daphne Major were studied by Peter and Rosemary Grant for 40+ years. During a severe drought in 1977, plants produced mostly large, hard seeds. Finches with larger, deeper beaks survived better than those with smaller beaks. (a) What type of selection is this? (b) If wet years returned and small seeds became abundant again, what might happen? (c) If the climate alternates between drought and wet years every 5 years, what long-term pattern would you predict for beak size?
workedExamples.solution: (a) This is directional selection — the population mean for beak size shifted toward larger beaks because larger-beaked birds survived the drought better. The Grants measured this: average beak depth increased by about 5% in one generation. This was one of the first direct observations of natural selection in action. (b) During wet years with abundant small seeds, the selection pressure reverses: smaller-beaked birds can eat small seeds more efficiently, so beak size might decrease. This is oscillating directional selection. (c) With regular 5-year drought/wet cycles, you'd expect the population to oscillate between larger and smaller beaks — never settling at one optimum.
