But if the swings are too fast, the population might evolve an intermediate beak that works reasonably well in both conditions (stabilizing selection), or beaks might become more variable overall (disruptive selection). This real example shows that evolution can occur rapidly — measurable change in a single generation.
workedExamples.explanation: The Grants' finch study is a landmark in evolutionary biology because it demonstrated natural selection occurring in real time — measurable within years, not millions of years. It showed that evolution can be both rapid and directional when selection pressures are strong. The key lesson: evolution is happening all around us, constantly, every generation.

commonMistakes: Thinking evolution is 'just a theory' — in science, a theory is the highest level of explanation, supported by mountains of evidence from genetics, paleontology, comparative anatomy, and direct observation. Evolution is as well-established as germ theory or plate tectonics.

Believing individuals evolve — individuals don't evolve, populations do. A giraffe doesn't 'grow a longer neck' during its lifetime; populations of giraffes with slightly longer necks (due to genetic variation) survive better and reproduce more, shifting the average neck length over generations.

Confusing 'survival of the fittest' with 'survival of the strongest' — fitness in evolution means reproductive success, not physical strength. A fungus that produces millions of spores is incredibly 'fit' in evolutionary terms, even though it's not strong or fast.

Thinking evolution has a goal or direction — evolution doesn't aim for 'higher' or 'more complex' organisms. It simply favors traits that increase survival and reproduction in a given environment. Bacteria are just as 'evolved' as humans — they've been evolving successfully for 3.5 billion years.

nodes.name: Neuroscience & Brain Function
nodes.description: How does your brain learn, remember, and think? Explore the biology of neurons, the chemistry of neurotransmitters, the architecture of the brain, and the neuroscience behind learning, memory, sleep, and decision-making. Understand the machine you're using to understand everything else.
keyConcepts: Neurons: structure (dendrites, axon, synapse), action potentials, and how they communicate
Brain regions: prefrontal cortex (decision-making, planning), hippocampus (memory formation), amygdala (emotion, fear), cerebellum (motor control)
Synaptic plasticity and Hebbian learning: 'neurons that fire together, wire together' — the cellular basis of learning
Long-term potentiation (LTP): how repeated stimulation strengthens synapses, forming lasting memories
Memory systems: working memory (limited, ~7 items), episodic memory (events), semantic memory (facts), procedural memory (skills)
Sleep and memory consolidation: how the brain replays and strengthens memories during sleep, especially REM and slow-wave sleep
Neuroplasticity: the brain's ability to reorganize itself throughout life, forming new neural connections
nodes.whyItMatters: Neuroscience is the ultimate meta-skill: understanding how your brain learns teaches you how to learn more effectively. Spaced repetition works because of long-term potentiation. Sleep before a test works because of memory consolidation. Focus works because of the prefrontal cortex's limited processing capacity. This lesson gives you the biological foundation for every learning technique you use in Learn-v1 — you'll understand why they work, not just that they work. The same knowledge applies to habit formation, productivity, decision-making, and mental health.
practiceProblems: A student crams for 6 hours the night before an exam, gets 5 hours of sleep, and performs poorly despite studying hard. Another student studies 1 hour per day for 6 days with good sleep and performs better. Explain the neuroscience behind this difference, referencing at least three concepts from this lesson.
Explain why spaced repetition (reviewing material at increasing intervals) is effective for long-term memory. What specific cellular and molecular mechanism does it exploit?

A person with damage to the hippocampus can learn to play a new piano piece (their hands learn) but has no conscious memory of ever having practiced. What does this tell us about the different memory systems in the brain?

Caffeine blocks adenosine receptors. Adenosine accumulates throughout the day and makes you feel sleepy. Why does caffeine make you feel more alert, and why does it affect sleep quality if consumed in the afternoon?

resources.title: Neuroscience Online (UT Houston)
resources.whyHelpful: University of Texas's complete online neuroscience textbook — covers everything from molecular neurobiology to complex brain systems. Free and comprehensive.

resources.title: How We Learn (Stanislas Dehaene)
resources.whyHelpful: Cognitive neuroscientist Dehaene's book on the four pillars of learning: attention, active engagement, error feedback, and consolidation. Directly applicable to how you use Learn-v1.

resources.title: The Power of Neuroplasticity (Sentis)
resources.whyHelpful: A beautiful animated explainer on neuroplasticity — how the brain rewires itself throughout life. Essential viewing for understanding that intelligence can grow.

resources.title: Khan Academy: Neural Systems
resources.whyHelpful: Khan Academy's section on the nervous system covering neuron structure, action potentials, and synaptic transmission with clear visuals and practice questions.

resources.title: Memory: The Hidden Pathways That Make Us Human
resources.whyHelpful: A documentary on the neuroscience of memory from the Royal Institution — covers how memories are formed, stored, and retrieved, including case studies of patients with extraordinary memory.

quiz.question: What is long-term potentiation (LTP) and why is it important for learning?
options: A temporary increase in blood flow to the brain during exercise
A lasting strengthening of synapses following repeated stimulation — the cellular basis of memory formation
The process of neurons dying and being replaced by new ones
A psychological state of heightened focus during studying
quiz.explanation: Long-term potentiation is the persistent strengthening of synapses based on recent patterns of activity. When a presynaptic neuron repeatedly stimulates a postsynaptic neuron, the connection becomes more efficient — this is often summarized as 'neurons that fire together, wire together.' LTP is the cellular mechanism underlying learning and memory formation, and it's why spaced repetition (repeated activation at intervals) is so effective for long-term retention.

quiz.question: A student pulls an all-nighter before a final exam, studying continuously without sleep. Based on neuroscience, why is this likely counterproductive?
options: The brain shuts down after 16 hours of wakefulness and refuses to form new memories
Sleep is required for memory consolidation — during sleep, the brain replays and strengthens recently learned information, transferring it from short-term to long-term memory
Cramming without sleep works fine as long as you drink enough caffeine
Only REM sleep matters for memory, and you can get enough REM in a 2-hour nap
quiz.explanation: Sleep is critical for memory consolidation. During slow-wave sleep, the hippocampus replays the day's experiences, transferring them to the cortex for long-term storage. During REM sleep, the brain integrates new information with existing knowledge. Without sleep, this consolidation process is severely impaired — you may have 'studied' for 8 hours but retained very little. This is why spaced study across multiple days with good sleep is far more effective than a single cram session.

quiz.question: The prefrontal cortex (PFC) has been called the 'CEO of the brain.' Which of the following is NOT a primary function of the PFC?
options: Controlling impulses and inhibiting irrelevant information
Storing long-term memories for facts and events
quiz.explanation: The prefrontal cortex is responsible for executive functions: planning, decision-making, impulse control, working memory, and goal-directed behavior. Long-term memory storage for facts and events (semantic and episodic memory) is primarily handled by the hippocampus and distributed throughout the cortex. The PFC uses information from long-term memory to make decisions, but it doesn't store that information long-term.

workedExamples.problem: Maria studies Spanish vocabulary using two methods. Method A: she reviews 20 words once per week for 4 weeks. Method B: she reviews the same 20 words for 30 minutes on four consecutive days, then stops. Three months later, Maria is tested on both sets. Method A words are remembered at 85% accuracy; Method B at 30%. Explain the neuroscience behind this difference. What specific cellular mechanism explains the result, and what real-world learning technique should Maria adopt?
workedExamples.solution: Method A (spaced repetition across weeks) triggers long-term potentiation (LTP) multiple times with rest intervals. Each review session reactivates the same neural circuits, and the intervening sleep periods allow for consolidation — the synaptic connections are strengthened each time. With Method B (massed practice), Maria activates the circuits intensely for 4 days but never gives her brain the spaced reactivation needed to convert short-term synaptic changes into stable, long-term connections. The cellular mechanism: repeated spaced activation of NMDA receptors in the hippocampus triggers a cascade of second messengers that lead to gene expression and new protein synthesis — physically building stronger synapses. By day 4 of massed practice, further stimulation produces diminishing returns (the 'ceiling effect' of LTP). Maria should adopt spaced repetition (like Anki or the spaced repetition system built into Learn-v1): review vocabulary at expanding intervals (1 day, 3 days, 7 days, 14 days, 30 days). Each review at the edge of forgetting produces the strongest LTP signal.
workedExamples.explanation: This example demonstrates the superiority of spaced repetition over cramming at the neural level. Spaced repetition works because it repeatedly triggers LTP at optimal intervals, with sleep providing the consolidation window. The brain literally builds stronger physical connections for information it retrieves multiple times across days and weeks.

workedExamples.problem: Tom has an important presentation tomorrow. He has two choices: (1) stay up late rehearsing, get 4 hours of sleep, and rehearse once more in the morning, or (2) stop rehearsing by 9 PM, get 8 hours of sleep, and do a single 20-minute review in the morning. Based on the neuroscience of sleep and memory, which choice will lead to a better performance? Explain using at least three specific brain processes.
workedExamples.solution: Choice 2 (good sleep + short morning review) will produce dramatically better performance. Three brain processes: (1) During slow-wave sleep (deep sleep), the hippocampus replays the day's neural activity patterns — essentially 'rehearsing' the presentation while you sleep. This replay strengthens the neural circuits encoding your speech content and delivery. (2) During REM sleep, the brain integrates the new presentation material with existing knowledge networks in the cortex, helping you make creative connections and handle unexpected questions. (3) Sleep clears adenosine (the molecule that accumulates during wakefulness and causes sleepiness), restoring the prefrontal cortex's ability to focus, regulate emotions, and retrieve information under stress — all critical for a confident presentation. Additionally, sleep deprivation suppresses hippocampal function, making it harder to retrieve the material you did learn. The 20-minute morning review reactivates the consolidated circuits, refreshing access to the material. Choice 1 not only loses these consolidation benefits but actively impairs retrieval ability.
workedExamples.explanation: Sleep isn't passive rest for the brain — it's an active memory consolidation process. The old saying 'sleep on it' has a real neurological basis. For any important cognitive task, optimizing sleep is the single most effective performance enhancer.

workedExamples.problem: A smartphone app designer wants to help users build a daily meditation habit. Using neuroscience principles, design the habit-building strategy. Address: (1) How dopamine affects motivation and habit formation, (2) How the basal ganglia and prefrontal cortex interact during habit formation, and (3) How neuroplasticity enables habit change over time.
workedExamples.solution: (1) Dopamine and motivation: The app should trigger small dopamine releases by providing immediate, predictable rewards after each meditation session — a visual streak counter, a calming sound effect, and a '5-day streak!' milestone notification. Dopamine reinforces the behavior by creating a positive association. Crucially, dopamine is released in anticipation of reward, so a reminder notification before the scheduled session builds anticipation. However, the app should use variable rewards (occasional bonus content, surprise insights) to prevent habituation, as unpredictable rewards produce stronger dopamine responses. (2) Basal ganglia and prefrontal cortex: Initially, the prefrontal cortex drives the habit — the user must consciously decide and commit to meditating (executive control). Over 2-4 weeks of consistent practice, the behavior shifts to the basal ganglia (specifically the striatum), which can execute the habit automatically without conscious effort. The app should provide 21-66 days of daily reminders (the range varies by habit complexity) to support this transition. Once the habit is automatic, it requires minimal willpower to maintain. (3) Neuroplasticity: Every meditation session strengthens the neural circuits involved in focus and emotional regulation through LTP. Over months, gray matter increases in the prefrontal cortex (attention, self-regulation) and decreases in the amygdala (stress reactivity) — this has been confirmed by MRI studies of long-term meditators. The app should visualize progress not just as days completed, but as a 'neural growth' indicator that reflects these gradual structural changes, giving users a sense of the deeper biological transformation occurring.
workedExamples.explanation: Habit formation is a biological process, not just a motivational one. Understanding the neuroscience — dopamine rewards, basal ganglia automation, and neuroplasticity — allows us to design environments that make desired habits inevitable rather than relying on willpower alone. The app designs the environment; the user's brain does the rest.

commonMistakes: Believing we only use 10% of our brain — this is a myth with no scientific basis. Brain imaging shows activity across virtually all brain regions throughout the day. Different tasks use different networks, but the whole brain is active.

Thinking neuroplasticity only happens in childhood — the adult brain remains plastic throughout life. New neurons are generated in the hippocampus (neurogenesis), and existing neurons form new connections in response to learning, exercise, and enriched environments. You can learn new skills, form new habits, and rewire your brain at any age.

Confusing correlation with causation in brain imaging research — when a brain region 'lights up' during a task, it means the region is active, not necessarily that it's causing the behavior. Brain regions participate in many functions; fMRI shows correlation, not causation.

Believing learning styles (visual, auditory, kinesthetic) are scientifically valid — they are a neuromyth. There is no evidence that matching instruction to a 'learning style' improves outcomes. The most effective learning strategies are active recall, spaced repetition, and interleaving — regardless of your preferred learning modality.

nodes.name: Fluid Dynamics
nodes.description: How liquids and gases move. From airplane wings to blood flow to weather patterns — the Navier-Stokes equations describe the motion of every fluid on Earth.
nodes.whyItMatters: Fluid dynamics explains why planes fly, how blood flows through your arteries, why weather patterns form, and how oil moves through pipelines. The Navier-Stokes equations are one of the Millennium Prize Problems — we use them every day but can't even prove they always have smooth solutions. In finance, 'market liquidity' is literally modeled as a fluid: order flow is like fluid flow, and market impact is like drag.
practiceProblems: Calculate the Reynolds number for water flowing through a pipe at 2 m/s
Explain why an airplane wing generates lift using Bernoulli's principle
Why does a curve ball curve? (Magnus effect)
Estimate the drag force on a car at 60 mph

resources.title: Khan Academy: Fluid Dynamics
resources.whyHelpful: Khan Academy's complete fluid dynamics course: pressure, Bernoulli's principle, viscosity, and flow. Clear visual explanations.

resources.title: Crash Course: Fluids
resources.whyHelpful: Quick, engaging overview of fluid mechanics. Good for building intuition before diving into equations.

resources.title: NASA: Beginner's Guide to Aerodynamics
resources.whyHelpful: NASA's educational resource on aerodynamics. Covers lift, drag, and the physics of flight with interactive simulations.

resources.title: 3Blue1Brown: Differential Equations (Fluids)
resources.whyHelpful: Visual introduction to the differential equations that govern fluid flow. Connects the math to the physics beautifully.

quiz.question: What does Bernoulli's principle state?
options: Density is constant in all flows
quiz.explanation: Bernoulli's principle: in a flowing fluid, an increase in velocity occurs simultaneously with a decrease in pressure. This is why airplane wings generate lift — air moves faster over the curved top surface, creating lower pressure above.

quiz.question: What is the Reynolds number used for?
options: Predicting whether flow is laminar or turbulent
quiz.explanation: Reynolds number (Re) = ρvL/μ. Low Re (< 2000) = laminar (smooth) flow. High Re (> 4000) = turbulent (chaotic) flow. It's the most important dimensionless number in fluid dynamics.

quiz.question: What is the difference between laminar and turbulent flow?
options: Laminar is smooth and orderly; turbulent is chaotic and mixing
quiz.explanation: Laminar flow: fluid moves in smooth, parallel layers with minimal mixing. Turbulent flow: chaotic, irregular motion with eddies and vortices. Turbulent flow has much higher drag and energy dissipation.

quiz.question: Why is solving the Navier-Stokes equations considered one of the hardest problems in mathematics?
options: We can't prove they always have smooth solutions — singularities might form
quiz.explanation: The Navier-Stokes existence and smoothness problem is a Millennium Prize Problem ($1M prize). We use these equations daily for weather prediction and aircraft design, but we can't mathematically prove that smooth solutions always exist in 3D. A singularity (blow-up) might form — we don't know.

workedExamples.problem: Water flows through a pipe of diameter 0.1 m at 2 m/s. Water density = 1000 kg/m³, viscosity = 0.001 Pa·s. Calculate the Reynolds number and determine if the flow is laminar or turbulent.
workedExamples.solution: Re = ρvD/μ = (1000 × 2 × 0.1) / 0.001 = 200,000. Since Re > 4000, the flow is turbulent. This means the water is mixing chaotically, not flowing in smooth layers. In practical terms: turbulent flow requires more pumping power (higher energy cost) but mixes better (useful in heat exchangers).
workedExamples.explanation: Reynolds number is the key parameter in fluid dynamics. It tells you everything about the flow regime without solving the full Navier-Stokes equations. Engineers use it to design pipes, aircraft, ships, and even artificial hearts.

workedExamples.problem: An airplane wing has air flowing at 60 m/s over the top surface and 50 m/s under the bottom. Air density = 1.2 kg/m³. Using Bernoulli's equation, estimate the pressure difference and lift per unit area.
workedExamples.solution: Bernoulli: P + ½ρv² = constant. Top: P_top + ½(1.2)(60)² = P_top + 2160. Bottom: P_bottom + ½(1.2)(50)² = P_bottom + 1500. Pressure difference: P_bottom - P_top = 2160 - 1500 = 660 Pa. Lift per unit area = 660 N/m². For a wing area of 50 m²: total lift = 660 × 50 = 33,000 N — enough to lift a 3,300 kg aircraft.
workedExamples.explanation: This is a simplified explanation of lift (the full story involves circulation and the Kutta condition). But Bernoulli's principle captures the essential physics: faster air on top means lower pressure, creating upward force.

workedExamples.problem: Blood flows through the aorta (diameter 2.5 cm) at 30 cm/s. Blood density = 1060 kg/m³, viscosity = 0.0035 Pa·s. Calculate Re. If the aorta narrows to 1.5 cm due to plaque, what happens to the flow velocity and pressure?
workedExamples.solution: Re = (1060 × 0.30 × 0.025) / 0.0035 = 2,271. This is in the transitional regime. Narrowed section: continuity equation A₁v₁ = A₂v₂. A₁/A₂ = (2.5/1.5)² = 2.78. So v₂ = 2.78 × 30 = 83.3 cm/s. Bernoulli: higher velocity means lower pressure in the narrowed section. This is the Venturi effect — the pressure drop can cause the artery to collapse slightly.
workedExamples.explanation: This is directly relevant to understanding atherosclerosis (plaque buildup in arteries). The narrowing increases velocity and decreases pressure, which can accelerate plaque formation — a dangerous feedback loop.

commonMistakes: Thinking Bernoulli's principle is the complete explanation of lift — it's a simplification; circulation and the Kutta condition are also needed
Confusing viscosity with density — viscosity is resistance to flow, density is mass per volume
Assuming turbulent flow is always bad — turbulence enhances mixing and heat transfer, which is useful in many applications
Ignoring the continuum assumption — Navier-Stokes assumes fluid is continuous, which breaks down at very small scales

nodes.name: Statistical Mechanics
nodes.description: How the microscopic behavior of atoms creates the macroscopic world you see. Temperature, pressure, and entropy emerge from the statistics of countless particles.
keyConcepts: Boltzmann distribution: probability of energy states
Entropy as a measure of disorder (S = k ln W)
Ideal gas law from first principles
nodes.whyItMatters: Statistical mechanics explains why ice melts, why heat flows from hot to cold, and why you can't unscramble an egg. It connects the microscopic world of atoms to the macroscopic world you experience. The concept of entropy — the tendency of systems to become more disordered — is one of the deepest ideas in all of science. In finance, statistical mechanics models are used to describe market behavior: price movements emerge from the 'microscopic' actions of individual data thinkers.
practiceProblems: Calculate the entropy change when 1 kg of ice melts at 0°C (L_f = 334 kJ/kg)
Derive the ideal gas law from kinetic theory
Explain why the Boltzmann distribution has an exponential form
Calculate the most probable speed of nitrogen molecules at 300K

resources.title: Khan Academy: Thermodynamics
resources.whyHelpful: Khan Academy's thermodynamics course covers the laws of thermodynamics, entropy, and heat engines with clear explanations.

resources.title: MIT OCW: Statistical Mechanics (8.333)
resources.whyHelpful: MIT's graduate-level statistical mechanics course. Rigorous treatment of Boltzmann distribution, partition functions, and phase transitions.

resources.title: 3Blue1Brown: Information Theory
resources.whyHelpful: Beautiful visual introduction to information theory, which is deeply connected to statistical mechanics through the concept of entropy.

resources.title: Feynman Lectures: Statistical Mechanics
resources.whyHelpful: Feynman's legendary lectures on statistical mechanics. Clear, deep, and full of physical insight.

quiz.question: What does the Boltzmann distribution tell us?
options: All particles have the same energy
The probability of a state decreases exponentially with its energy
Temperature is constant in all systems
quiz.explanation: Boltzmann distribution: P(E) ∝ e^(-E/kT). Higher energy states are exponentially less probable. At higher temperatures, higher energy states become more accessible.

quiz.question: What is entropy, fundamentally?
options: A measure of the number of microstates corresponding to a macrostate (S = k ln W)
quiz.explanation: Boltzmann's entropy formula: S = k ln W, where W is the number of microstates. More microstates = higher entropy = more disorder. The second law says entropy tends to increase because there are more disordered states than ordered ones.

quiz.question: Why does heat flow from hot to cold objects?
options: Because there are far more microstates where energy is spread evenly — it's a statistical certainty
quiz.explanation: When two objects touch, energy can be distributed in many more ways when it's spread evenly. There are vastly more microstates corresponding to the 'both warm' macrostate. So the system almost certainly evolves toward equal temperature.

quiz.question: What is a phase transition?
options: A sudden change in macroscopic properties as a control parameter changes smoothly
A change in the number of particles
quiz.explanation: Phase transitions (melting, boiling, freezing) are sudden changes in macroscopic properties. At the critical temperature, the system reorganizes: ice crystals break apart (melting), liquid molecules escape (boiling).

workedExamples.problem: Calculate the entropy change when 1 kg of ice at 0°C melts into water at 0°C. Latent heat of fusion L_f = 334 kJ/kg.
workedExamples.solution: ΔS = Q/T = mL_f/T = (1 × 334,000) / 273.15 = 1223 J/K. The entropy increases because liquid water has more microstates (more ways to arrange molecules) than ice (rigid crystal lattice).
workedExamples.explanation: Entropy is not just 'disorder' — it's precisely quantifiable. The entropy of fusion (1223 J/kg·K for water) reflects the difference in molecular freedom between solid and liquid phases.

workedExamples.problem: Derive the ideal gas law (PV = nRT) from kinetic theory. Consider N molecules of mass m in a volume V, each with average kinetic energy ½mv² = 3/2 kT.
workedExamples.solution: A molecule hitting a wall: momentum change Δp = 2mv_x. Time between collisions: Δt = 2L/v_x. Force from one molecule: F = mv_x²/L. Total force: F = Nm⟨v_x²⟩/L. By isotropy: ⟨v_x²⟩ = ⟨v²⟩/3. Pressure: P = Nm⟨v²⟩/(3V). Using ½m⟨v²⟩ = 3/2 kT: P = NkT/V. Since Nk = nR: PV = nRT.
workedExamples.explanation: This derivation shows that the ideal gas law emerges naturally from the statistical behavior of countless molecules. Temperature is literally the average kinetic energy of molecules.

workedExamples.problem: Nitrogen molecules (mass = 4.65 × 10⁻²⁶ kg) at 300K. Calculate the most probable speed using the Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution. k = 1.38 × 10⁻²³ J/K.
workedExamples.solution: v_mp = sqrt(2kT/m) = sqrt(2 × 1.38×10⁻²³ × 300 / 4.65×10⁻²⁶) = sqrt(1.78×10⁵) = 422 m/s. Average speed = 476 m/s. RMS speed = 517 m/s. Most molecules move at roughly 400-500 m/s at room temperature — about 1500 km/h!
workedExamples.explanation: The Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution tells us not just the average speed, but the full distribution. Some molecules are nearly stationary, others move twice as fast as average.

commonMistakes: Thinking entropy means 'disorder' in the colloquial sense — entropy is precisely S = k ln W, counting microstates
Confusing temperature with heat — temperature is average kinetic energy per particle; heat is total thermal energy transferred
Thinking the second law is absolute — entropy can decrease locally (e.g., freezing), but total entropy always increases
Ignoring the statistical nature of thermodynamics — the second law is statistical, not deterministic

nodes.name: Molecular Biology & Biochemistry
nodes.description: Life at the molecular level. DNA, proteins, enzymes, and the chemical reactions that make life possible. Understand the machinery inside every cell.
keyConcepts: Protein synthesis and the genetic code
ATP: the energy currency of cells
Cellular respiration: glycolysis, Krebs cycle, electron transport
Photosynthesis: converting light to chemical energy
nodes.whyItMatters: Molecular biology is the foundation of modern medicine, biotechnology, and agriculture. Understanding how DNA codes for proteins explains genetic diseases, cancer, and aging. CRISPR gene editing is revolutionizing medicine — we can now cut and paste DNA like editing a document. The same information theory that powers your phone (bits, codes, error correction) applies to DNA: it's a 4-letter code (A,T,G,C) with built-in error correction.
practiceProblems: Given a DNA sequence, write the mRNA and amino acid sequence
Calculate the ATP yield from oxidizing one glucose molecule
Explain how an enzyme lowers activation energy
Design a CRISPR guide RNA to edit a specific gene

resources.title: Khan Academy: Molecular Biology
resources.whyHelpful: Khan Academy's complete biology course covering DNA, RNA, proteins, and cellular processes with interactive exercises.

resources.title: Crash Course: Biology
resources.whyHelpful: Engaging video series covering all of biology. The molecular biology episodes are particularly well done.

resources.title: iBiology: Molecular Biology Videos
resources.whyHelpful: Free lectures by leading biologists. Covers cutting-edge research in molecular biology, gene regulation, and biotechnology.

resources.title: Nature: CRISPR Explained
resources.whyHelpful: Nature's accessible guide to CRISPR gene editing — how it works, what it can do, and the ethical implications.

quiz.question: What is the central dogma of molecular biology?
quiz.explanation: The central dogma: DNA is transcribed into RNA, which is translated into protein. Information flows from DNA to RNA to protein.

quiz.question: What is the role of ATP in cells?
options: Energy currency — ATP stores and transfers energy for cellular processes
quiz.explanation: ATP (adenosine triphosphate) is the universal energy currency. When ATP → ADP + P, energy is released to power cellular processes. Your body recycles its entire weight in ATP every day.

quiz.question: How do enzymes speed up chemical reactions?
options: By lowering the activation energy barrier
quiz.explanation: Enzymes are biological catalysts that lower the activation energy of reactions. They don't change the equilibrium — they just help the system reach equilibrium faster.

quiz.question: What does CRISPR-Cas9 do?
options: Cuts DNA at specific locations, allowing genes to be edited
quiz.explanation: CRISPR-Cas9 is a molecular scissors: a guide RNA directs the Cas9 enzyme to a specific DNA sequence, where it makes a cut. The cell's repair machinery then fixes the cut, allowing genes to be deleted, inserted, or replaced.

workedExamples.problem: Given the DNA coding strand: 5'-ATGCGATCG-3'. Write the mRNA sequence and the amino acid sequence.
workedExamples.solution: mRNA (same as coding strand but with U instead of T): 5'-AUGCGAUCG-3'. Codons: AUG CGA UCG. AUG = Met (start codon), CGA = Arg, UCG = Ser. Amino acid sequence: Met-Arg-Ser.
workedExamples.explanation: The genetic code is a triplet code: 3 nucleotides = 1 amino acid. With 4 nucleotides, there are 4³ = 64 possible codons, coding for 20 amino acids plus stop signals.

workedExamples.problem: Calculate the total ATP yield from the complete oxidation of one glucose molecule (C₆H₁₂O₆) in cellular respiration.
workedExamples.solution: Glycolysis: 2 ATP + 2 NADH. Pyruvate oxidation: 2 NADH. Krebs cycle: 2 GTP + 6 NADH + 2 FADH₂. ETC: each NADH → ~2.5 ATP, each FADH₂ → ~1.5 ATP. Total: 2 + 2 + 5 + 5 + 15 + 3 = 32 ATP per glucose. Theoretical maximum is 36-38 ATP.
workedExamples.explanation: Cellular respiration is remarkably efficient: 32 ATP × 30.5 kJ/mol = 976 kJ captured from glucose's 2870 kJ total energy. Efficiency = 34%, far better than any engine.

workedExamples.problem: An enzyme catalyzes a reaction with Km = 10 μM and Vmax = 100 μM/min. Using the Michaelis-Menten equation v = Vmax[S]/(Km + [S]), calculate the reaction rate at [S] = 5 μM, 10 μM, and 50 μM.
workedExamples.solution: At [S] = 5 μM: v = 100 × 5 / (10 + 5) = 33.3 μM/min. At [S] = 10 μM (= Km): v = 100 × 10 / 20 = 50 μM/min = Vmax/2. At [S] = 50 μM: v = 100 × 50 / 60 = 83.3 μM/min. The curve is hyperbolic: rate increases quickly at low [S] but saturates at high [S].
workedExamples.explanation: Michaelis-Menten kinetics is the foundation of enzyme biology. Km tells you the enzyme's affinity for its substrate (lower Km = higher affinity). Vmax tells you the maximum catalytic rate.

commonMistakes: Confusing the coding strand with the template strand — mRNA is complementary to the template strand
Thinking enzymes change reaction equilibrium — they only speed up reaching equilibrium
Ignoring that ATP yield varies by cell type — the 32 ATP figure assumes the malate-aspartate shuttle
Thinking CRISPR is perfect — off-target effects are a major concern in therapeutic applications

nodes.name: Earth Science & Climate
nodes.description: How our planet works: plate tectonics, the carbon cycle, ocean currents, and the climate system. Understand the science behind climate change and why it matters.
keyConcepts: Plate tectonics: continental drift, subduction, and seafloor spreading
The carbon cycle: sources, sinks, and feedback loops
The greenhouse effect: how CO₂ traps heat
Climate sensitivity: doubling CO₂ → how much warming?
Paleoclimate: what ice cores and sediments tell us
Climate models: how scientists predict the future
nodes.whyItMatters: Climate change is the defining challenge of the 21st century. Understanding the science — the carbon cycle, feedback loops, and climate sensitivity — is essential for making informed decisions. The same systems thinking applies to markets: feedback loops, tipping points, and emergent behavior appear in both climate and finance.
practiceProblems: Calculate the radiative forcing from doubling CO₂ (ΔF = 5.35 × ln(C/C₀) W/m²)
Explain the ice-albedo feedback loop
If climate sensitivity is 3°C per doubling of CO₂, how much warming occurs when CO₂ goes from 420 to 840 ppm?
Describe how thermohaline circulation affects European climate

resources.title: Khan Academy: Earth Science
resources.whyHelpful: Khan Academy's earth science and cosmology courses cover plate tectonics, climate, and Earth's history.

resources.title: NASA: Climate Change Evidence
resources.whyHelpful: NASA's comprehensive resource on climate change evidence — temperature records, sea level rise, ice loss, and extreme events.

resources.title: IPCC: Climate Change Reports
resources.whyHelpful: The Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change reports are the authoritative scientific consensus on climate change.

resources.title: 3Blue1Brown: Climate Modeling
resources.whyHelpful: Visual explanation of how climate models work — from basic physics to complex simulations.

quiz.question: What is the greenhouse effect?
options: CO₂ reflects sunlight back to space
Greenhouse gases absorb and re-emit infrared radiation, trapping heat in the atmosphere
The ozone layer blocks UV radiation
quiz.explanation: The greenhouse effect: sunlight passes through the atmosphere and warms the surface. The surface emits infrared radiation. Greenhouse gases absorb and re-emit this IR radiation, trapping heat.

quiz.question: What is climate sensitivity?
options: How sensitive people are to climate change
The equilibrium temperature increase from doubling atmospheric CO₂
The rate of CO₂ increase per year
quiz.explanation: Climate sensitivity ≈ 3°C per doubling of CO₂ (range: 2.5-4°C). This is one of the most important numbers in climate science.

quiz.question: What is the ice-albedo feedback loop?
options: Ice reflects less sunlight than water
Melting ice reduces Earth's reflectivity, causing more warming, which melts more ice — a positive feedback loop
quiz.explanation: Ice has high albedo (reflects 80% of sunlight). Ocean water has low albedo (absorbs 90%). When ice melts, more sunlight is absorbed, warming the planet, which melts more ice.

quiz.question: What drives thermohaline circulation (the 'ocean conveyor belt')?
options: Differences in water temperature and salinity (density)
quiz.explanation: Thermohaline circulation is driven by density differences: cold, salty water is dense and sinks. Warm water flows north to replace it, keeping Europe 5-10°C warmer than it would otherwise be.

workedExamples.problem: Calculate the radiative forcing from CO₂ increasing from 280 ppm (pre-industrial) to 420 ppm (current). Use ΔF = 5.35 × ln(C/C₀) W/m².
workedExamples.solution: ΔF = 5.35 × ln(420/280) = 5.35 × 0.405 = 2.17 W/m². This is the additional energy trapped by the atmosphere. Using climate sensitivity: ΔT = λ × ΔF, where λ ≈ 0.8 °C per W/m². ΔT = 0.8 × 2.17 = 1.7°C. We've observed ~1.2°C so far.
workedExamples.explanation: The logarithmic relationship means each doubling of CO₂ produces the same forcing. Going from 280→560 ppm produces the same ΔF as 560→1120 ppm.

workedExamples.problem: The Arctic has lost 40% of its summer sea ice since 1980. Ice albedo = 0.8, ocean albedo = 0.1. Solar radiation at the surface = 200 W/m² in summer. Calculate the additional energy absorbed due to ice loss over 1 million km².
workedExamples.solution: Energy absorbed by ice: 200 × (1 - 0.8) = 40 W/m². Energy absorbed by ocean: 200 × (1 - 0.1) = 180 W/m². Difference: 140 W/m². Over 1 million km²: 140 × 10¹² = 140 TW. For comparison: total human energy consumption ≈ 18 TW. The ice-albedo feedback absorbs 8x more energy than all of human civilization uses.
workedExamples.explanation: The ice-albedo feedback is one of the most dangerous climate tipping points. Once enough ice melts, the warming becomes self-reinforcing.

workedExamples.problem: Climate models predict 2-4°C warming by 2100 under current policies. If the world's economy is $100 trillion and climate damages are estimated at 10% of GDP per degree of warming, calculate the expected cost of 3°C warming. Compare to the cost of mitigation (transitioning to clean energy: estimated $50 trillion over 30 years).
workedExamples.solution: Climate damages at 3°C: 3 × 10% × $100 trillion = $30 trillion per year. Over 50 years: $1,500 trillion in cumulative damages. Mitigation cost: $50 trillion over 30 years. Benefit-cost ratio: 30:1. Even with heavy discounting, mitigation is far cheaper than adaptation.
workedExamples.explanation: This illustrates the core economic insight: climate change is the largest externality in human history. The cost of action is far less than the cost of inaction.

commonMistakes: Confusing weather with climate — weather is short-term and chaotic; climate is long-term averages and trends
Thinking CO₂ is the only greenhouse gas — methane (CH₄) is 80x more potent over 20 years
Ignoring feedback loops — the direct CO₂ warming is amplified by water vapor, ice-albedo, and cloud feedbacks
Thinking climate models are unreliable — they've accurately predicted warming since the 1970s
